title: Combining Clones and Single-Cell Objects
text: single-cell RNA sequencing workflows, dimensional reduction typically performed first identifying highly variable features. features used directly UMAP/tSNE projection inputs principal component analysis. approach commonly applied clustering well. However, immune-focused datasets, VDJ genes TCR BCR often among variable genes. variability arises naturally due clonal expansion diversity within lymphocytes. result, UMAP projections clustering outcomes may influenced clonal information rather broader transcriptional differences across cell types. mitigate issue, common strategy exclude VDJ genes set highly variable features proceeding clustering dimensional reduction. introduce set functions facilitate process removing VDJ-related genes either Seurat Object vector gene names (useful SCE-based workflows).

title: Combining Clones and Single-Cell Objects
text: quietVDJgenes() – Removes TCR BCR VDJ genes. quietTCRgenes() – Removes TCR VDJ genes. quietBCRgenes() – Removes BCR VDJ genes, retains BCR VDJ pseudogenes variable features. Let’s first check top 10 variable features scRep_example Seurat object removal: Now, ’ll remove TCR VDJ genes scRep_example object: applying functions, can ensure clustering dimensional reduction driven broader transcriptomic differences across cell types rather skewed inherent variability due clonal expansion. provides accurate representation cellular heterogeneity independent clonal lineage.
code: # Check the first 10 variable features before removal VariableFeatures(scRep_example)[1:10] ##  [1] "TRBV7-2"  "HSPA1B"   "HSPA1A"   "TRBV4-1"  "CCL4"     "TRBV5-1"  ##  [7] "TRBV10-3" "TRBV3-1"  "TRBV6-6"  "CD79A" # Remove TCR VDJ genes scRep_example <- quietTCRgenes(scRep_example)  # Check the first 10 variable features after removal VariableFeatures(scRep_example)[1:10] ##  [1] "HSPA1B"  "HSPA1A"  "CCL4"    "CD79A"   "HLA-DRA" "CCL20"   "TUBA1B"  ##  [8] "HSPA6"   "TNF"     "MS4A1"

title: Combining Clones and Single-Cell Objects
text: processing contig data clones via combineBCR() combineTCR(), can add clonal information single-cell object using combineExpression(). Importantly, major requirement attachment matching contig cell barcodes barcodes row names metadata Seurat Single-Cell Experiment object. match, attachment fail. suggest making changes single-cell object barcodes ease use.

title: Combining Clones and Single-Cell Objects
text: Part combineExpression() calculating clonal frequency proportion, placing clone groups called cloneSize. default cloneSize argument uses following bins: c(Rare = 1e-4, Small = 0.001, Medium = 0.01, Large = 0.1, Hyperexpanded = 1), can modified include /less bins different names. Clonal frequency proportion dependent repertoires compared. can modify calculation using group.parameter, grouping Patient variable. group.set, combineExpression() calculate clonal frequency, proportion, cloneSize function individual sequencing runs. Additionally, cloneSize can use frequency clones proportion = FALSE. Key Parameter(s) combineExpression() input.data: product combineTCR(), combineBCR(), list containing . sc.data: Seurat Single-Cell Experiment (SCE) object attach clonal data . proportion: TRUE (default), calculates proportion clone; FALSE, calculates total frequency. cloneSize: Bins grouping based proportion frequency. proportion FALSE cloneSize bins set high enough, upper limit automatically adjust. filterNA: Method subset Seurat/SCE object barcodes without clone information. addLabel: Adds label frequency header, useful trying multiple group.variables recalculating frequencies subsetting. can look default cloneSize groupings using Single-Cell Experiment object just created, group.set sample variable used combineTCR():  Alternatively, want cloneSize based frequency clone, can set proportion = FALSE need change cloneSize bins integers. haven’t inspected clone data, setting upper limit clonal frequency might difficult; combineExpression() automatically adjust upper limit fit distribution frequencies.
code: sce <- combineExpression(combined.TCR,                           sce,                           cloneCall="gene",                           group.by = "sample",                           proportion = TRUE)  #Define color palette  colorblind_vector <- hcl.colors(n=7, palette = "inferno", fixup = TRUE)  plotUMAP(sce, colour_by = "cloneSize") +     scale_color_manual(values=rev(colorblind_vector[c(1,3,5,7)])) scRep_example <- combineExpression(combined.TCR,                                     scRep_example,                                     cloneCall="gene",                                     group.by = "sample",                                     proportion = FALSE,                                     cloneSize=c(Single=1, Small=5, Medium=20, Large=100, Hyperexpanded=500))  Seurat::DimPlot(scRep_example, group.by = "cloneSize") +     scale_color_manual(values=rev(colorblind_vector[c(1,3,4,5,7)]))

BCR <- combineBCR(...) list.receptors <- c(TCR, BCR)   seurat <- combineExpression(list.receptors,                              seurat,                              cloneCall="gene",                              proportion = TRUE)

title: Quantifying Clonal Bias
text: excellent work Zhang et al. (2018, Nature), authors introduced new methods looking clones cellular origins cluster identification. strongly recommend read cite publication using function. use StartracDiversity() function, need output combineExpression() function column metadata specifies tissue origin.

title: Quantifying Clonal Bias
text: expa - Clonal Expansion. Measures degree clonal proliferation within given cell cluster. calculated 1 - normalized Shannon entropy, higher value indicates clones dominate cluster. migr - Cross-tissue Migration. Quantifies movement clonal T cells different tissues, defined type parameter. index based entropy single clonotype’s distribution across specified tissues. tran - State Transition. Measures developmental transition T cell clones different functional clusters. index calculated entropy single clonotype’s distribution across cell clusters identified data. Key Parameters StartracDiversity() type: variable metadata provides tissue type. group.: column header metadata group analysis (e.g., “sample”, “treatment”). default, StartracDiversity() calculate three indices (expa, migr, tran) cluster group define. provides comprehensive overview clonal dynamics dataset. output three-paneled plot, panel representing one index. example data, type corresponds “Type” column, includes “P” (peripheral blood) “L” (lung) classifiers. analysis grouped “Patient” column.
code: # Calculate and plot all three STARTRAC indices StartracDiversity(scRep_example,                    type = "Type",                    group.by = "Patient")

title: Quantifying Clonal Bias
text: ’re interested one aspect clonal dynamics, can specify using index parameter. example, calculate plot clonal expansion:
code: # Calculate and plot only the clonal expansion index StartracDiversity(scRep_example,                    type = "Type",                    group.by = "Patient",                   index = "expa")

title: Quantifying Clonal Bias
text: Another feature StartracDiversity() ability perform pairwise comparisons. specifically quantify migration two tissues (e.g., Lung vs. Periphery), set index = "migr" tell function column use comparison pairwise = "Type".
code: # # Calculate pairwise migration between tissues StartracDiversity(scRep_example,                    type = "Type",                    group.by = "Patient",                   index = "migr",                   pairwise = "Type")

title: Quantifying Clonal Bias
text: new metric proposed Massimo et al, clonalBias(), like STARTRAC, clonal metric seeks quantify individual clones skewed towards specific cellular compartment cluster. clone bias 1 indicates clone composed cells single compartment cluster, clone bias 0 matches background subtype distribution. Please read cite linked manuscript using clonalBias() Key Parameter(s) clonalBias() group.: column header metadata bias based . split.: variable use calculating baseline frequencies (e.g., “Type” lung vs peripheral blood comparison) n.boots: Number bootstraps downsample. min.expand: Clone frequency cut-purpose comparison (default = 10). calculate plot clonal bias using aa clone calls, splitting “Patient” grouping “seurat_clusters”, minimum expansion 5 10 bootstraps:
code: clonalBias(scRep_example,             cloneCall = "aa",             split.by = "Patient",             group.by = "seurat_clusters",            n.boots = 10,            min.expand =5)

title: Clustering by Edit Distance
text: clonalCluster() function provides powerful method group clonotypes based sequence similarity. calculates edit distance CDR3 sequences uses information build network, identifying closely related clusters T B cell receptors. functionality allows nuanced definition “clone” extends beyond identical sequence matches.

title: Clustering by Edit Distance
text: clustering process follows key steps: Sequence Selection: function selects either nucleotide (sequence = "nt") amino acid (sequence = "aa") CDR3 sequences specified chain. Distance Calculation: calculates edit distance pairs sequences. default, also requires sequences share V gene (use.v = TRUE). Network Construction: edge created two sequences meet similarity threshold, forming network graph. Clustering: graph-based clustering algorithm run identify connected components communities within network. default, identifies directly indirectly connected sequences single cluster (cluster.method = "components"). Output: function can either add resulting cluster IDs input object, return igraph object network analysis, export sparse adjacency matrix.

title: Clustering by Edit Distance
text: behavior threshold parameter critical controlling cluster granularity: Normalized Similarity (threshold < 1): threshold value 0 1 (e.g., 0.85), represents normalized edit distance (Levenshtein distance / mean sequence length). higher value corresponds stricter similarity requirement. useful comparing sequences varying lengths. Raw Edit Distance (threshold >= 1): threshold whole number (e.g., 2), represents maximum raw edit distance allowed. lower value corresponds stricter similarity requirement. useful want allow specific number mutations. Key Parameter(s) clonalCluster() sequence: Specifies whether cluster based aa (amino acid) nt (nucleotide) sequences. threshold: similarity threshold clustering. Values < 1 normalized similarity, values >= 1 raw edit distance. group.: column header metadata lists group analysis (e.g., “sample”, “treatment”). NULL, clusters calculated across sequences. use.V: TRUE, sequences must share V gene clustered together. use.J: TRUE, sequences must share J gene clustered together. cluster.method: clustering algorithm use. Defaults components, finds connected subgraphs. cluster.prefix: character prefix add cluster names (e.g., “cluster.”). exportGraph: TRUE, returns igraph object sequence network. exportAdjMatrix: TRUE, returns sparse adjacency matrix (dgCMatrix) network.

title: Clustering by Edit Distance
text: run clustering first two samples TRA chain, using amino acid sequences normalized similarity threshold 0.85:
code: # Run clustering on the first two samples for the TRA chain sub_combined <- clonalCluster(combined.TCR[c(1,2)],                                chain = "TRA",                                sequence = "aa",                                threshold = 0.85)  # View the new cluster column head(sub_combined[[1]][, c("barcode", "TCR1", "TRA.Cluster")]) ##                   barcode                     TCR1 TRA.Cluster ## 1 P17B_AAACCTGAGTACGACG-1       TRAV25.TRAJ20.TRAC        <NA> ## 2 P17B_AAACCTGCAACACGCC-1 TRAV38-2/DV8.TRAJ52.TRAC        <NA> ## 3 P17B_AAACCTGCAGGCGATA-1      TRAV12-1.TRAJ9.TRAC  cluster.18 ## 4 P17B_AAACCTGCATGAGCGA-1      TRAV12-1.TRAJ9.TRAC  cluster.18 ## 5 P17B_AAACGGGAGAGCCCAA-1        TRAV20.TRAJ8.TRAC cluster.159 ## 6 P17B_AAACGGGAGCGTTTAC-1      TRAV12-1.TRAJ9.TRAC  cluster.18

what: Demonstrating Clustering with a Single-Cell Object
title: Clustering by Edit Distance
text: can calculate clusters based specific metadata variables within single-cell object using group.parameter. useful analyzing clusters per-sample per-patient basis without subsetting data first. First, add patient type information scRep_example Seurat object: Now, run clustering scRep_example Seurat object, grouping calculations “Patient”:
code: #Adding patient information scRep_example$Patient <- substr(scRep_example$orig.ident, 1,3)  #Adding type information scRep_example$Type <- substr(scRep_example$orig.ident, 4,4) # Run clustering, but group calculations by "Patient" scRep_example <- clonalCluster(scRep_example,                                 chain = "TRA",                                 sequence = "aa",                                 threshold = 0.85,                                 group.by = "Patient")  #Define color palette  num_clusters <- length(unique(na.omit(scRep_example$TRA.Cluster))) cluster_colors <- hcl.colors(n = num_clusters, palette = "inferno")  DimPlot(scRep_example, group.by = "TRA.Cluster") +   scale_color_manual(values = cluster_colors, na.value = "grey") +    NoLegend()

title: Clustering by Edit Distance
text: Instead modifying input object, clonalCluster() can export underlying network structure advanced analysis. Set exportGraph = TRUE get igraph object consisting networks barcodes indicated clustering scheme.
code: set.seed(42) #Clustering Patient 19 samples igraph.object <- clonalCluster(combined.TCR[c(5,6)],                                chain = "TRB",                                sequence = "aa",                                group.by = "sample",                                threshold = 0.85,                                 exportGraph = TRUE)  # Setting color scheme col_legend <- factor(igraph::V(igraph.object)$group) col_samples <- hcl.colors(2,"inferno")[as.numeric(col_legend)] color.legend <- factor(unique(igraph::V(igraph.object)$group))  # Sampling 1000 Barcodes sample.vertices <- V(igraph.object)[sample(length(igraph.object), 1000)] subgraph.object <- induced_subgraph(igraph.object, vids = sample.vertices) V(subgraph.object)$degrees <- igraph::degree(subgraph.object) edge_alpha_color <- adjustcolor("gray", alpha.f = 0.3)  #Plotting plot(subgraph.object,      layout = layout_nicely(subgraph.object),      vertex.label = NA,      vertex.size = sqrt(igraph::V(subgraph.object)$degrees),       vertex.color = col_samples[sample.vertices],      vertex.frame.color = "white",       edge.color = edge_alpha_color,      edge.arrow.size = 0.05,      edge.curved = 0.05,       margin = -0.1) legend("topleft",         legend = levels(color.legend),         pch = 16,         col = unique(col_samples),         bty = "n")

title: Clustering by Edit Distance

title: Comparing Clonal Diversity and Overlap

title: Comparing Clonal Diversity and Overlap
text: Key Parameters clonalDiversity() metric: diversity metric calculate. x.axis: additional metadata variable group samples along x-axis. return.boots: TRUE, returns bootstrap values instead mean. skip.boots: TRUE, disables downsampling bootstrapping. n.boots: number bootstrap iterations perform (default 100). calculate Shannon diversity, grouped sample:  two options grouping clonalDiversity(): group.x.axis. group.: Reorganizes clone information new groups calculation based . x.axis: Keeps organization clone information , plots along x-axis improved visibility grouping. First, add “Patient” variable combined.TCR Now, calculate Inverse Simpson diversity, grouped “Patient”:  Calculate Inverse Simpson diversity “Patient” x-axis, keeping original grouping:  clonalDiversity() functions quantifying comparing diversity immune repertoires across different samples experimental conditions. implementing bootstrapping offering wide range diversity metrics, provides robust comparable measures clonal richness evenness, fundamental understanding immune responses disease states
code: clonalDiversity(combined.TCR,                  cloneCall = "gene") combined.TCR <- addVariable(combined.TCR,                              variable.name = "Patient",                               variables = c("P17", "P17", "P18", "P18",  "P19","P19", "P20", "P20")) clonalDiversity(combined.TCR,                  cloneCall = "gene",                  group.by = "Patient",                  metric = "inv.simpson") clonalDiversity(combined.TCR,                  cloneCall = "gene",                  x.axis = "Patient",                  metric = "inv.simpson")

what: clonalProportion: Examining Space Occupied by Ranks of Clones
title: Visualizing Clonal Dynamics
text: Like clonal space homeostasis, clonalProportion() also categorizes clones separate bins. key difference instead looking relative proportion clone total, clonalProportion() ranks clones total count frequency occurrence places predefined bins. clonalSplit parameter represents ranking clonotypes. example, 1:10 refers top 10 clonotypes sample. default bins set clonalSplit variable can adjusted. Default clonalSplit Bins 10 (top 1-10 clones) 100: (top 11-100 clones) 1000: (top 101-1000 clones) 10000: (top 1001-10000 clones) 30000: (top 10001-30000 clones) 100000: (top 30001-100000 clones) visualize clonal proportion using gene clone calls default clonalSplit bins:  visualize clonal proportion using nt (nucleotide) clone calls custom clonalSplit bins:  clonalProportion() complements clonalHomeostasis() providing perspective “richest” (abundant) clones contribute overall repertoire space. segmenting clones rank-based bins, helps identify whether highly expanded clones larger number moderately expanded clones dominate immune response, offering distinct insights repertoire structure dynamics.
code: clonalProportion(combined.TCR,                   cloneCall = "gene") clonalProportion(combined.TCR,                   cloneCall = "nt",                  clonalSplit = c(1, 5, 10, 100, 1000, 10000))

title: Basic Clonal Visualizations
text: clonalLength() allows look length distribution CDR3 sequences. Importantly, unlike basic visualizations, cloneCall can nt (nucleotide) aa (amino acid). Due method calling clones outlined previously (e.g., using NA unreturned chain sequences multiple chains within single barcode), length distribution may reveal multimodal curve. visualize amino acid length distribution chains (“”):  visualize amino acid length distribution TRA chain, scaled density plot:
code: clonalLength(combined.TCR,               cloneCall="aa",               chain = "both") clonalLength(combined.TCR,               cloneCall="aa",               chain = "TRA",               scale = TRUE)

what: clonalCompare: Clonal Dynamics Between Categorical Variables
title: Basic Clonal Visualizations
text: clonalCompare() allows look clones samples changes dynamics. useful tracking proportions top clones change conditions. Key Parameters clonalCompare() samples: character vector isolate specific samples list element name. clones: character vector specific clonal sequences visualize. used, top.clones ignored. *top.clones: top n number clones graph, calculated based frequency within individual samples. highlight.clones: character vector specific clonal sequences color; clones greyed . relabel.clones: TRUE, simplifies legend labeling isolated clones numerically (e.g., “Clone: 1”). graph: type plot generate; alluvial (default) area. proportion: TRUE (default), y-axis represents proportional abundance; FALSE, represents raw clone counts. compare top 10 clones samples “P17B” “P17L” using amino acid sequences alluvial plot:  can also choose highlight specific clones, case “CVVSDNTGGFKTIF_CASSVRRERANTGELFF” “NA_CASSVRRERANTGELFF” using highlight.clones parameter. addition, can simplify plot label clones “Clone: 1”, “Clone: 2”, etc., setting relabel.clones = TRUE.  Alternatively, want show specific clones, can use clones parameter.
code: clonalCompare(combined.TCR,                    top.clones = 10,                    samples = c("P17B", "P17L"),                    cloneCall="aa",                    graph = "alluvial") clonalCompare(combined.TCR,                top.clones = 10,               highlight.clones = c("CVVSDNTGGFKTIF_CASSVRRERANTGELFF", "NA_CASSVRRERANTGELFF"),               relabel.clones = TRUE,               samples = c("P17B", "P17L"),                cloneCall="aa",                graph = "alluvial") clonalCompare(combined.TCR,                clones = c("CVVSDNTGGFKTIF_CASSVRRERANTGELFF", "NA_CASSVRRERANTGELFF"),               relabel.clones = TRUE,               samples = c("P17B", "P17L"),                cloneCall="aa",                graph = "alluvial")

title: Basic Clonal Visualizations
text: clonalScatter() organizes two repertoires, quantifies relative clone sizes, produces scatter plot comparing two samples. Clones categorized counts singlets expanded, either exclusively present shared selected samples. Key Parameter(s) clonalScatter() x.axis, y.axis: Names list elements meta data variable place x-axis y-axis. dot.size: Specifies dot size determined; total (default) displays total number clones x- y-axis, specific list element name size calculation. graph: type graph display; proportion relative proportion clones (default) count total count clones sample. compare samples “P18B” “P18L” based gene clone calls, dot size representing total number clones, plotting clone proportions:
code: clonalScatter(combined.TCR,                cloneCall ="gene",                x.axis = "P18B",                y.axis = "P18L",               dot.size = "total",               graph = "proportion")

title: Combining Contigs into Clones
text: combineTCR() function processes list TCR sequencing results, consolidating level individual cell barcodes. handles potential issues repeated barcodes adding prefixes samples ID parameters. output includes combined reads clone calls nucleotide sequence (CTnt), amino acid sequence (CTaa), VDJC gene sequence (CTgene), combination nucleotide gene sequence (CTstrict). Key Parameter(s) combineTCR() input.data: list filtered contig annotations (e.g., filtered_contig_annotations.csv 10x Cell Ranger) outputs loadContigs(). samples: Labels samples (recommended). ID: Additional sample labels (optional). removeNA: TRUE, removes cell barcode NA value least one chain (default FALSE). removeMulti: TRUE, removes cell barcode two immune receptor chains (default FALSE). filterMulti: TRUE, isolates top two expressed chains cell barcodes multiple chains (default FALSE). filterNonproductive: TRUE, removes non-productive chains variable exists contig data (default TRUE). combine TCR contigs contig_list apply sample prefixes: combineTCR() essential first step organizing raw TCR sequencing data structured format scRepertoire analyses. allows flexible handling single paired chains, barcode disambiguation, initial filtering, producing list data frames row represents single cell associated TCR clonotypes.

title: Combining Contigs into Clones
text: combineBCR() function primary tool processing raw B cell receptor contig data format ready analysis. analogous combineTCR() includes specialized logic handling complexities BCRs, somatic hypermutation. function consolidates contigs single data frame per sample, row representing unique cell. default (call.related.clones = TRUE), combineBCR() groups B cells clones based similarity CDR3 sequences.

title: Combining Contigs into Clones
text: Internally calling clonalCluster() build network related sequences. Using threshold parameter define connections. threshold normalized Levenshtein distance, value closer 1.0 requires higher sequence similarity. default 0.85 good starting point. Assigning cluster-based ID CTstrict column. Additionally, group.argument allows constrain clustering analysis occur within distinct categories metadata. example, using group.= "sample" ensures sequences different samples never compared clustered together, even identical. Key Parameter(s) combineBCR() call.related.clones: TRUE (default), uses clonalCluster() identify related clones based sequence similarity. group.: column header used group clones clustering (NULL, clusters calculated across samples). threshold: similarity threshold clonalCluster() (default: 0.85). dist_type: metric calculating difference: “levenshtein” (default), “hamming”, “nw” (Needleman-Wunsch), “sw” (Smith-Waterman). dist_mat: substitution matrix used dist_type “nw” “sw” (e.g., “BLOSUM62”, “PAM30”) normalize: handle threshold: “none”, “length”, “maxlen”. chain: chain use clustering call.related.clones = TRUE (default: ). sequence: sequence type (nt aa) clustering (default: nt). use.V, use.J: TRUE, sequences must share V/J gene clustered (default: TRUE) cluster.method: clustering algorithm apply edit-distanc network (default: components). First, load example BCR contig data: , combine BCR contigs using default similarity clustering:
code: # Load example BCR contig data BCR.contigs <- read.csv("https://www.borch.dev/uploads/contigs/b_contigs.csv") # Combine using the default similarity clustering combined.BCR.clustered <- combineBCR(BCR.contigs,                                       samples = "Patient1",                                       threshold = 0.85)  # The CTstrict column contains cluster IDs (e.g., "cluster.1") head(combined.BCR.clustered[[1]][, c("barcode", "CTstrict", "IGH", "cdr3_aa1")]) ## title: Combining Contigs into Clones
text: combineBCR() includes several arguments filter clean contig data processing. * filterNonproductive = TRUE (Default): Removes contigs classified productive, ensuring functional receptor chains included analysis. * filterMulti = TRUE (Default): cells one heavy light chain detected, automatically selects chain highest UMI count (read abundance) discards others. helps resolve cellular multiplets technical artifacts. example applying filters (though default): combineBCR() designed processing B cell repertoire data, going beyond simple contig aggregation incorporate advanced clustering based CDR3 sequence similarity. enables identification clonally related B cells, crucial studying B cell development, affinity maturation, humoral immune responses. filtering options ensure quality interpretability processed data.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: Depending pipeline used generate single-cell object, may inherent mismatches barcodes single-cell object output combineBCR() combineTCR().

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: Seurat-modified barcodes: default, Seurat append _X suffix barcodes. original: ACGTACGTACGTACGT-1 seurat-modified: ACGTACGTACGTACGT-1_1 scRepertoire-modified barcodes: scRepertoire uses samples /ID parameters combineTCR() combineBCR() add prefix barcodes. original: ACGTACGTACGTACGT-1 scRepertoire-modified: Sample1_ACGTACGTACGTACGT-1

what: Solution: Renaming Cell Barcodes in Seurat
title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: easiest way make compatible rename cell barcodes Seurat object using RenameCells() SeuratObject package
code: # Assuming 'seuratObj' is your Seurat object cell.barcodes <- rownames(seuratObj[[]]) # removing the _1 at the end of the barcodes (adjust regex if your suffix differs) cell.barcodes <- stringr::str_split(cell.barcodes, "_", simplify = TRUE)[,1] # adding the prefix of the orig.ident to the barcodes, assuming that is the sample IDs cell.barcodes <- paste0(seuratObj$orig.ident, "_", cell.barcodes) seuratObj <- RenameCells(seuratObj, new.names = cell.barcodes)

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: visualizations scRepertoire, two primary ways adjust color scheme. Methods Color Adjustment Internal Palette Selection: Change palette parameter within scRepertoire functions desired color scheme. approach uses built-palettes grDevices, can access list available color schemes using hcl.pals(). Adding ggplot Layer: Extend scRepertoire plot (ggplot object) adding ggplot2 layer new color scheme using scale_fill_manual() similar functions.

title: Frequently Asked Questions
code: # Internal Palette Selection clonalQuant(combined.TCR,              cloneCall="strict",              chain = "both",              scale = TRUE,              palette = "Zissou 1")

title: Frequently Asked Questions
code: # Using gg System clonalQuant(combined.TCR,              cloneCall="strict",              chain = "both",              scale = TRUE) +    scale_fill_manual(values = hcl.colors(8,"geyser"))

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: Since scRepertoire functions return ggplot objects, modifying general appearance theme plot straightforward, similar adjusting color palettes—adding ggplot2 theme layer.
code: # Original clonalQuant plot clonalQuant(combined.TCR,              cloneCall="strict",              chain = "both",              scale = TRUE) # Modifying the theme of the clonalQuant plot clonalQuant(combined.TCR,              cloneCall="strict",              chain = "both",              scale = TRUE) +    theme_classic()

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: order grouping/group.variables scRepertoire plots (whether along axis color legends) can precisely controlled using order.parameter. Key Parameter Plot Order order.: character vector defining desired order elements group.variable. ’s crucial strings vector exactly match group.strings. Alternatively, setting order.= "alphanumeric" automatically sort groups alphanumerically.
code: clonalQuant(combined.TCR,              cloneCall="strict",              chain = "both",              scale = TRUE,              order.by = c("P17B","P18B","P19B","P20B","P17L","P18L","P19L","P20L"))

title: Frequently Asked Questions
text: Within general analysis functions scRepertoire, ’s option export underlying data frame used create visualization. Key Parameter Data Export exportTable: Set parameter TRUE return data frame used generate graph instead visual output.
code: clonalQuant_output <- clonalQuant(combined.TCR,                                    cloneCall="strict",                                    scale = TRUE,                                    exportTable = TRUE) clonalQuant_output ##   contigs values total   scaled ## 1     745   P17B  2805 26.55971 ## 2    2117   P17L  2893 73.17663 ## 3    1254   P18B  1328 94.42771 ## 4    1202   P18L  1278 94.05321 ## 5    5544   P19B  6942 79.86171 ## 6    1619   P19L  2747 58.93702 ## 7    6087   P20B  8991 67.70103 ## 8     192   P20L   201 95.52239

title: Combining Deep Learning and BCRs with Ibex
text: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) Pros: Detect local sequence motifs effectively; relatively straightforward quick train. Cons: Can struggle capture global context Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) Pros: Model sequences within probabilistic, continuous latent space; suitable generating novel variants. Cons: Training can complex (balancing reconstruction regularization losses); interpretability may less direct. choose? Use CNNs local motif detection simpler training priorities. Use VAEs want generative model capturing broader sequence structures.

title: Combining Deep Learning and BCRs with Ibex
text: One-Hot Encoding: Represents amino acid binary vector (e.g., 20-length vector 20 standard residues). Pros: Simple assumption-free. Cons: High-dimensional doesn’t capture biochemical similarities. Atchley Factors: Uses five numerical descriptors summarizing key physicochemical properties. Pros: Compact embeds biochemical information. Cons: May overlook residue-specific nuances. Cruciani Properties: Encodes amino acids via descriptors reflect molecular shape, hydrophobicity, electronic features. Pros: Captures rich chemical details. Cons: complex compute less standardized. Kidera Factors: Provides ten orthogonal values derived broad set physical chemical properties. Pros: Offers balanced, low-dimensional representation. Cons: Derived statistically, potentially averaging finer details. MSWHIM: Derives descriptors 3D structural data, summarizing overall shape surface properties. Pros: Provides robust, rotation-invariant structural insight. Cons: Requires 3D information can computationally intensive. tScales: Encodes amino acids based topological structural features reflective protein folding interactions. Pros: Captures contextual information overall sequence structure. Cons: Less commonly used, making standardization tool support challenge.

Another option CoNGA, produces embedding using BCR RNA. Ibex designed make customizable approach combined approach using R.

title: Combining Deep Learning and BCRs with Ibex
text: Ibex includes two primary functions: Ibex_matrix() runIbex(). Ibex_matrix() function serves backbone algorithm, returning encoded values based user-selected parameters. contrast runIbex(), filters input include B cells attached BCR data, Ibex_matrix() operates provided data. Additionally, compatible list output combineBCR() function (scRepertoire package), whereas runIbex() designed use single-cell object.

title: Combining Deep Learning and BCRs with Ibex
text: "Heavy" Ig Heavy Chain "Light" Ig Light Chain "encoder": Applies CNN/VAE-based transformation. "geometric": Uses geometric transformation. "CNN": CDR3 Convolutional Neural Network-based autoencoder "VAE": CDR3 Variational Autoencoder "CNN.EXP": CDR1/2/3 CNN "VAE.EXP": CDR1/2/3 VAE "atchleyFactors" "crucianiProperties" "kideraFactors" "MSWHIM" "tScales" "OHE" theta: geometric transformation, defines value theta (default π/3).
code: Ibex_vectors <- Ibex_matrix(ibex_example,                              chain = "Heavy",                             method = "encoder",                             encoder.model = "VAE",                              encoder.input = "OHE",                              species = "Mouse",                             verbose = FALSE) ## 1/7 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 22ms/step2/7 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 1ms/step                                    7/7 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 3ms/step ggplot(data = as.data.frame(Ibex_vectors), aes(Ibex_1, Ibex_2)) +    geom_point(color = "grey", alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +    theme_classic() Ibex_vectors2 <- Ibex_matrix(ibex_example,                               chain = "Heavy",                              method = "geometric",                              geometric.theta = pi,                               verbose = FALSE)  ggplot(as.data.frame(Ibex_vectors2), aes(x = Ibex_1, y = Ibex_2)) +    geom_point(color = "grey", alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +    theme_classic()

title: Combining Deep Learning and BCRs with Ibex

code: ibex_example <- runIbex(ibex_example,                          chain = "Heavy",                         encoder.input = "kideraFactors",                          reduction.name = "Ibex.KF",                          species = "Mouse",                         verbose = FALSE) ## 1/7 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 20ms/step                                     7/7 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 2ms/step

title: Combining Deep Learning and BCRs with Ibex
text: runIbex() encoded values stored “Ibex…”. Using Ibex dimensions, can calculate UMAP based solely embedded heavy chain values. visualize Heavy/Light Chain amino acid sequence (via CTaa) normalized counts associated Anti-Hen-Egg-Lysozyme antigen.  workflow, can combine three dimension reductions single, integrated UMAP embedding using runMultiUMAP() function cosine metric. refine integration, apply rescaleByNeighbors() align nearest neighbors across modalities, followed clustering clusterRows(), resulting “combined.clustering” reflects data types. Finally, visualize joint embedding “MultiUMAP,” coloring points expression specific protein marker (e.g., Anti-Hen-Egg-Lysozyme), integrated cluster assignments, relevant annotations. result holistic representation cellular diversity leverages shared unique signals RNA, protein, Ibex IGH latent features.
code: set.seed(123) #Generating UMAP from Ibex Neighbors ibex_example <- runUMAP(ibex_example,                          dimred = "Ibex.KF",                         name = "ibexUMAP") #Ibex UMAP plot1 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example, color_by ="Anti-Hen-Egg-Lysozyme", dimred = "ibexUMAP") +              theme(legend.position = "bottom") plot2 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example, color_by = "CTaa", dimred = "ibexUMAP") +    scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE, option = "B") +    guides(color = "none")  plot1 + plot2 #Multimodal UMAP ibex_example <- mumosa::runMultiUMAP(ibex_example,                                       dimreds=c("pca", "apca", "Ibex.KF")) #Multimodal Clustering output <- rescaleByNeighbors(ibex_example,                               dimreds=c("pca", "apca", "Ibex.KF")) ibex_example$combined.clustering <- clusterRows(output, NNGraphParam())  plot3 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example,                    dimred = "MultiUMAP",                    color_by = "Anti-Hen-Egg-Lysozyme") +              theme(legend.position = "bottom") plot4 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example,                    dimred = "MultiUMAP",                    color_by = "combined.clustering") +              theme(legend.position = "bottom") plot5 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example,                    dimred = "MultiUMAP",                    color_by = "CTaa") +    scale_color_manual(values = viridis_pal(option = "B")(length(unique(ibex_example$CTaa)))) +   guides(color = "none")  plot3 + plot4 + plot5

code: ibex_example <- runUMAP(ibex_example,                          dimred = 'pca',                          name = "pcaUMAP")  ibex_example <- runUMAP(ibex_example,                          dimred = 'apca',                          name = "beamUMAP")  plot6 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example,                    dimred = "pcaUMAP",                    color_by = "combined.clustering")  plot7 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example,                    dimred = "beamUMAP",                    color_by = "combined.clustering")  plot8 <- plotUMAP(ibex_example,                    dimred = "ibexUMAP",                    color_by = "combined.clustering")   plot6 + plot7 + plot8 + plot_layout(guides = "collect") &     theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Combining Deep Learning and BCRs with Ibex
text: Single-cell B-cell receptor (BCR) sequencing enables identification clonotypes, groups B cells sharing BCR sequence. Often, want link clonotypes gene expression profiles. challenge arises, however, clonotype contains multiple cells (e.g., 10 cells sharing BCR). Including cells every clonotype can lead -representation highly expanded clones complicate analyses require one--one mapping clonotypes “cells.” Recent work Schattgen,2021 proposed different strategies summarize represent clonotype single expression profile. Two key strategies common: Distance Approach First, look PCA count matrices Identify cell minimum summed Euclidean distance cells clonotype. approach can help ensure single representation actual cell, rather potentially non-biological average. Mean Approach Simply take average (mean) expression across cells clonotype. Conceptually, collapse multi-cell clone one “virtual cell” representing average expression.
code: CoNGA.sce <- CoNGAfy(ibex_example,                       method = "mean",                       assay = c("RNA", "BEAM"))  CoNGA.sce <- runIbex(CoNGA.sce,                       encoder.input = "kideraFactors",                       encoder.model = "VAE",                      reduction.name = "Ibex.KF",                       species = "Mouse",                      verbose = FALSE) ## 1/2 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 20ms/step                                     2/2 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 12ms/step CoNGA.sce <- CoNGA.sce %>%                   runUMAP(dimred = "Ibex.KF",                            name = "ibexUMAP")  plot9 <- plotUMAP(CoNGA.sce,                    dimred = "ibexUMAP",                    color_by = "Anti-Hen-Egg-Lysozyme",                    by.assay.type = "counts")   plot10 <- plotUMAP(CoNGA.sce,                     dimred = "ibexUMAP",                     color_by = "H5N1",                     by.assay.type = "counts")   plot9 + plot10 &    theme(legend.position = "bottom")

code: S1 <- read.csv(".../Sample1/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv") S2 <- read.csv(".../Sample2/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv") S3 <- read.csv(".../Sample3/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv") S4 <- read.csv(".../Sample4/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv")  contig_list <- list(S1, S2, S3, S4)

title: Loading Data into scRepertoire
text: Beyond default 10x Genomics Cell Ranger pipeline outputs, scRepertoire supports various single-cell immune receptor sequencing formats loadContigs() function.

what: Supported Formats and Expected File Names
title: Loading Data into scRepertoire
text: 10X: “filtered_contig_annotations.csv” AIRR: “airr_rearrangement.tsv” BD: “Contigs_AIRR.tsv” Dandelion: “all_contig_dandelion.tsv” Immcantation: “_data.tsv” (similar) JSON: “.json” MiXCR: “clones.tsv” ParseBio: “barcode_report.tsv” TRUST4: “barcode_report.tsv” WAT3R: “barcode_results.csv” Key Parameter(s) loadContigs() input: directory path containing contig files (function recursively search) list/data frame pre-loaded contig data. format: string specifying data format (e.g., 10X, TRUST4, WAT3R). set “auto”, function attempt automatically detect format based file names data structure. can provide loadContigs() directory sequencing experiments located, recursively load process contig data based file names: Alternatively, loadContigs() can given list pre-loaded data frames process contig data based specified format:
code: # Directory example contig.output <- c("~/Documents/MyExperiment") contig.list <- loadContigs(input = contig.output,                             format = "TRUST4") # List of data frames example S1 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample1/outs/barcode_results.csv") S2 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample2/outs/barcode_results.csv") S3 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample3/outs/barcode_results.csv") S4 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample4/outs/barcode_results.csv")  contig.list <- list(S1, S2, S3, S4) contig.list <- loadContigs(input = contig.list,                             format = "WAT3R")

title: Additional Processing Steps
text: Likewise, can remove specific list elements combineTCR() combineBCR() using subsetClones() function. order subset, need identify column header like use subsetting (name) specific values include (variables). title: Additional Processing Steps
text: assigning clone barcode, can export clonal information using exportClones() save later use integrate bioinformatics pipelines. function supports various output formats tailored different analytical needs. Key Parameter(s) exportClones() * format: desired output format clonal data. * airr: Exports data Adaptive Immune Receptor Repertoire (AIRR) Community-compliant format, row representing single receptor chain. * immunarch: Exports list containing data frame metadata formatted use immunarch package. * paired: Exports data frame paired chain information (amino acid, nucleotide, genes) per barcode. default. * TCRMatch: Exports data frame specifically TCRMatch algorithm, containing TRB chain amino acid sequence clonal frequency. * tcrpheno: Exports data frame compatible tcrpheno pipeline, TRA TRB chains separate columns. * write.file: TRUE (default), saves output CSV file. FALSE, returns data frame list R environment. * dir: directory output file saved. Defaults current working directory. * file.name: name CSV file saved. export combined clonotypes paired data frame save specified directory: return immunarch-formatted data frame directly R environment without saving file:
code: exportClones(combined,               write.file = TRUE,              dir = "~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample1/"              file.name = "clones.csv") immunarch <- exportClones(combined.TCR,                            format = "immunarch",                            write.file = FALSE) head(immunarch[[1]][[1]]) ##   Clones   Proportion                                             CDR3.nt ## 1      1 0.0003565062    NA;TGCGCCAGCAGTCGGGGACTAGCGGGATACAATGAGCAGTTCTTC ## 2      1 0.0003565062       NA;TGTGCCATCAGCGCGGACCCCCGCTACAATGAGCAGTTCTTC ## 3      1 0.0003565062 NA;TGTGCCAGCAGCTTGAGGGACAGCTATCGGTACTATGGCTACACCTTC ## 4      2 0.0007130125          NA;TGTGCCAGCAGCCGGCAGGGCGCAGATACGCAGTATTTT ## 5      1 0.0003565062       NA;TGTGCCAGCAGTCCCTTTACAGGGTTCTATGGCTACACCTTC ## 6      1 0.0003565062          NA;TGTGCCAGCTCATCCGGGATCAATCAGCCCCAGCATTTT ##               CDR3.aa      V.name  D.name     J.name   C.name ## 1  NA;CASSRGLAGYNEQFF NA;TRBV10-2 NA;None NA;TRBJ2-1 NA;TRBC2 ## 2   NA;CAISADPRYNEQFF NA;TRBV10-3 NA;None NA;TRBJ2-1 NA;TRBC2 ## 3 NA;CASSLRDSYRYYGYTF NA;TRBV11-3 NA;None NA;TRBJ1-2 NA;TRBC1 ## 4    NA;CASSRQGADTQYF NA;TRBV11-3 NA;None NA;TRBJ2-3 NA;TRBC2 ## 5   NA;CASSPFTGFYGYTF NA;TRBV12-4 NA;None NA;TRBJ1-2 NA;TRBC1 ## 6    NA;CASSSGINQPQHF   NA;TRBV18 NA;None NA;TRBJ1-5 NA;TRBC1 ##                                           Barcode ## 1                         P17B_AGCGGTCCAAAGGAAG-1 ## 2                         P17B_GGCTCGAGTCGCGGTT-1 ## 3                         P17B_CGCGTTTTCGGCTACG-1 ## 4 P17B_CACCAGGGTTCCTCCA-1;P17B_TCTATTGCAGGTGCCT-1 ## 5                         P17B_GCTGGGTGTACGAAAT-1 ## 6                         P17B_AGGGTGACATTGGTAC-1

what: clonalBin: Bin Clones by Frequency or Proportion
title: Additional Processing Steps
text: clonalBin() function adds clonal grouping variable (cloneSize) output combineTCR(), combineBCR(), combineExpression(). function calculates clonal frequency proportion, bins clones categories based customizable thresholds. useful categorizing clones prior downstream analysis visualization, without needing attach data single-cell object. Key Parameter(s) clonalBin() clone.call: Defines clonal sequence grouping (gene, nt, aa, strict). chain: TCR/BCR chain use (, TRA, TRB, TRG, TRD, IGH, IGL). group.: column header metadata group analysis (e.g., “sample”, “Type”). proportion: Whether use proportion (TRUE) total frequency (FALSE) binning. clone.size: bins grouping based proportion frequency.

previous_headings: clonalBin: Bin Clones by Frequency or Proportion
title: Additional Processing Steps
text: default, clonalBin() uses proportion-based binning following default bins: c(Rare = 1e-4, Small = 0.001, Medium = 0.01, Large = 0.1, Hyperexpanded = 1).
code: combined.TCR <- clonalBin(combined.TCR, clone.call = "strict")  # Check the cloneSize column head(combined.TCR[[1]][, c("barcode", "CTstrict", "clonalProportion", "clonalFrequency", "cloneSize")]) ##                   barcode ## 1 P17B_AGCGGTCCAAAGGAAG-1 ## 2 P17B_GGCTCGAGTCGCGGTT-1 ## 3 P17B_CGCGTTTTCGGCTACG-1 ## 4 P17B_CACCAGGGTTCCTCCA-1 ## 5 P17B_TCTATTGCAGGTGCCT-1 ## 6 P17B_GCTGGGTGTACGAAAT-1 ##                                                                             CTstrict ## 1    NA;NA_TRBV10-2.None.TRBJ2-1.TRBC2;TGCGCCAGCAGTCGGGGACTAGCGGGATACAATGAGCAGTTCTTC ## 2       NA;NA_TRBV10-3.None.TRBJ2-1.TRBC2;TGTGCCATCAGCGCGGACCCCCGCTACAATGAGCAGTTCTTC ## 3 NA;NA_TRBV11-3.None.TRBJ1-2.TRBC1;TGTGCCAGCAGCTTGAGGGACAGCTATCGGTACTATGGCTACACCTTC ## 4          NA;NA_TRBV11-3.None.TRBJ2-3.TRBC2;TGTGCCAGCAGCCGGCAGGGCGCAGATACGCAGTATTTT ## 5          NA;NA_TRBV11-3.None.TRBJ2-3.TRBC2;TGTGCCAGCAGCCGGCAGGGCGCAGATACGCAGTATTTT ## 6       NA;NA_TRBV12-4.None.TRBJ1-2.TRBC1;TGTGCCAGCAGTCCCTTTACAGGGTTCTATGGCTACACCTTC ##   clonalProportion clonalFrequency                  cloneSize ## 1     0.0003565062               1 Small (1e-04 < X <= 0.001) ## 2     0.0003565062               1 Small (1e-04 < X <= 0.001) ## 3     0.0003565062               1 Small (1e-04 < X <= 0.001) ## 4     0.0007130125               2 Small (1e-04 < X <= 0.001) ## 5     0.0007130125               2 Small (1e-04 < X <= 0.001) ## 6     0.0003565062               1 Small (1e-04 < X <= 0.001) # View the distribution of clone sizes table(combined.TCR[[1]]$cloneSize) ##  ## Hyperexpanded (0.1 < X <= 1)      Large (0.01 < X <= 0.1)  ##                          952                          784  ##   Medium (0.001 < X <= 0.01)   Small (1e-04 < X <= 0.001)  ##                          340                          729  ##        Rare (0 < X <= 1e-04)             None ( < X <= 0)  ##                            0                            0

title: Additional Processing Steps
text: using frequency-based binning (proportion = FALSE), clone.size values must integers. maximum frequency exceeds upper bin limit, automatically adjusted.
code: combined.TCR.freq <- combineTCR(contig_list,                                 samples = c("P17B", "P17L", "P18B", "P18L",                                             "P19B","P19L", "P20B", "P20L"))  combined.TCR.freq <- clonalBin(combined.TCR.freq,                                clone.call = "strict",                                proportion = FALSE,                                clone.size = c(Rare = 1, Small = 5, Medium = 20,                                               Large = 100, Hyperexpanded = 500))  # Check the frequency-based binning head(combined.TCR.freq[[1]][, c("barcode", "clonalFrequency", "cloneSize")]) ##                   barcode clonalFrequency          cloneSize ## 1 P17B_AGCGGTCCAAAGGAAG-1               1  Rare (0 < X <= 1) ## 2 P17B_GGCTCGAGTCGCGGTT-1               1  Rare (0 < X <= 1) ## 3 P17B_CGCGTTTTCGGCTACG-1               1  Rare (0 < X <= 1) ## 4 P17B_CACCAGGGTTCCTCCA-1               2 Small (1 < X <= 5) ## 5 P17B_TCTATTGCAGGTGCCT-1               2 Small (1 < X <= 5) ## 6 P17B_GCTGGGTGTACGAAAT-1               1  Rare (0 < X <= 1)

title: Additional Processing Steps
text: can calculate clonal frequency proportion across samples grouped variable (e.g., “Type” “sample”). particularly useful want consistent binning across experimental conditions.

text: annotateInvariant() function enables identification mucosal-associated invariant T (MAIT) cells invariant natural killer T (iNKT) cells single-cell sequencing datasets. specialized T-cell subsets defined characteristic TCR usage, making distinguishable within single-cell immune profiling data. function extracts TCR chain information provided single-cell dataset evaluates known invariant TCR criteria either MAIT iNKT cells. cell assigned score indicating presence (1) absence (0) specified invariant T-cell population. Key Parameter(s) annotateInavriant() type: Character string specifying type invariant T cell annotate (MAIT iNKT). species: Character string specifying species (mouse human).
code: combined <- annotateInvariant(combined,                                type = "MAIT",                                species = "human")  combined <- annotateInvariant(combined,                               type = "iNKT",                               species = "human")

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: Quantify proportion amino acids along CDR3 sequence percentAA(). default, function pad sequences NAs maximum aa.length. Sequences longer aa.length removed visualization (default aa.length = 20). Key Parameter(s) percentAA() aa.length: maximum length CDR3 amino acid sequence consider. visualize relative percentage amino acids position CDR3 sequences TRB chain, length 20 amino acids:  plot displays relative proportion amino acid different positions across CDR3 sequence specified chain. provides insights amino acid composition variability residue, can indicative functional constraints selection pressures CDR3
code: percentAA(combined.TCR,            chain = "TRB",            aa.length = 20)

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: can also quantify level entropy/diversity across amino acid residues along CDR3 sequence. positionalEntropy() combines quantification residue percentAA() diversity calculations. Positions without variance value reported 0 purposes comparison. Key Parameter(s) positionalEntropy() shannon - Shannon Index inv.simpson - Inverse Simpson Index gini.simpson - Gini-Simpson Index norm.entropy - Normalized Entropy pielou - Pielou’s Evenness hill1, hill2, hill3 - Hill Numbers visualize normalized entropy across amino acid residues TRB chain, length 20 amino acids:  plot generated positionalEntropy() illustrates diversity entropy amino acid position within CDR3 sequence. Higher entropy values indicate greater variability amino acid usage position, suggesting less selective pressure promiscuous binding, lower values suggest conserved positions critical structural integrity antigen recognition.
code: positionalEntropy(combined.TCR,                    chain = "TRB",                    aa.length = 20)

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: Like positionalEntropy(), can also examine series amino acid properties along CDR3 sequences using positionalProperty(). Important differences positionalProperty() dropping NA values void mean calculation displaying ribbon 95% confidence interval surrounding mean value selected properties. Key Parameter(s) positionalEntropy() atchleyFactors: citation crucianiProperties: citation FASGAI: citation kideraFactors: citation MSWHIM: citation ProtFP: citation stScales: citation tScales: citation VHSE: citation zScales: citation examine Atchley factors amino acids across CDR3 sequence first two samples:  function provides detailed view physicochemical properties amino acids change along CDR3 sequence. visualizing mean property value confidence interval, one can identify positions distinct characteristics significant variations groups, offering insights structural functional aspects CDR3.
code: positionalProperty(combined.TCR[c(1,2)],                    chain = "TRB",                    aa.length = 20,                    method = "atchleyFactors") +    scale_color_manual(values = hcl.colors(5, "inferno")[c(2,4)])

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: Gene quantification visualization redesigned offer robust translatable function percentGeneUsage(). maintained functionality previous functions gene-level quantification aliases vizGenes(), percentGenes(), percentVJ().

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: vizGenes() function offers highly adaptable approach visualizing relative usage TCR BCR genes. acts versatile alias percentGeneUsage(), allowing comparisons across different chains, scaling values, selection bar charts heatmaps. x.axis: Specifies gene segment display along x-axis (e.g., “TRBV”, “TRBD”, “IGKJ”). Another gene segment (e.g., “TRAV”, “TRBJ”) paired gene analysis. x.axis y.axis gene segments, vizGenes() internally calls percentGeneUsage() genes = c(x.axis, y.axis), resulting heatmap. categorical variable (e.g., “sample”, “orig.ident”) visualize gene usage across different groups. y.axis categorical variable, vizGenes() maps group.parameter percentGeneUsage(), creating facets category. “barplot”: Ideal visualizing distribution single gene segment. “heatmap”: Suitable single gene usage (group.y.axis categorical variable) paired gene analysis. summary.fun: (Inherited percentGeneUsage) Defines statistic display: “percent” (default), “proportion”, “count”. implicitly handles scaling values.  plot shows proportion TRBV gene segment observed across entire combined.TCR dataset. Since y.axis NULL, samples grouped list element combined.TCR data. vizGenes() particularly useful examining gene pairings. Let’s look differences TRBV TRBJ usage peripheral blood lung samples dataset. ’ll subset combined.TCR analysis.   examples, providing x.axis y.axis gene segments (“TRBV” “TRBJ”), vizGenes() automatically performs paired gene analysis, generating heatmap intensity reflects percentage V-J pairing. Beyond V-J pairings within single chain, vizGenes() can also visualize gene usage across different chains. instance, examine TRBV TRAV pairings patient P17’s samples:
code: vizGenes(combined.TCR,          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = NULL, # No specific y-axis variable, will group all samples          plot = "barplot",          summary.fun = "proportion") # Peripheral Blood Samples vizGenes(combined.TCR[c("P17B", "P18B", "P19B", "P20B")],          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = "TRBJ",          plot = "heatmap",          summary.fun = "percent") # Display percentages # Lung Samples vizGenes(combined.TCR[c("P17L", "P18L", "P19L", "P20L")],          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = "TRBJ",          plot = "heatmap",          summary.fun = "percent") # Display percentages vizGenes(combined.TCR[c("P17B", "P17L")],          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = "TRAV",          plot = "heatmap",          summary.fun = "count")

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: percentGenes() function specialized alias percentGeneUsage() designed quantify usage single V, D, J gene locus specified immune receptor chain. default, returns heatmap visualization. Key Parameters percentGenes(): chain: Specifies immune receptor chain (e.g., “TRB”, “TRA”, “IGH”, “IGL”). gene: Indicates gene locus quantify: “Vgene”, “Dgene”, “Jgene”. group., order., summary.fun, exportTable, palette: parameters directly passed percentGeneUsage(). quantify visualize percentage TRBV gene usage across samples:  generates heatmap showing percentage TRBV gene segment within sample, allowing easy visual comparison gene usage profiles across samples. raw data returned percentGenes() (exportTable = TRUE) can powerful input downstream analysis, dimensionality reduction techniques like Principal Component Analysis. allows summarize complex gene usage patterns identify samples similar distinct repertoires.  PCA plot visually clusters samples based TRBV gene usage profiles, helping identify underlying patterns relationships samples.
code: percentGenes(combined.TCR,              chain = "TRB",              gene = "Vgene",              summary.fun = "percent") df.genes <- percentGenes(combined.TCR,                          chain = "TRB",                          gene = "Vgene",                          exportTable = TRUE,                          summary.fun = "proportion")   # Performing PCA on the gene usage matrix pc <- prcomp(t(df.genes))  # Getting data frame to plot from df_plot <- as.data.frame(cbind(pc$x[,1:2], colnames(df.genes))) colnames(df_plot) <- c("PC1", "PC2", "Sample") df_plot$PC1 <- as.numeric(df_plot$PC1) df_plot$PC2 <- as.numeric(df_plot$PC2)  ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2)) +   geom_point(aes(fill = Sample), shape = 21, size = 5) +   guides(fill=guide_legend(title="Samples")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = hcl.colors(nrow(df_plot), "inferno")) +   theme_classic() +   labs(title = "PCA of TRBV Gene Usage")

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: percentVJ() function another specialized alias percentGeneUsage(), specifically designed quantify proportion percentage V J gene segments paired together within individual repertoires. always produces heatmap visualization. Key Parameters percenVJ(): chain: Specifies immune receptor chain (e.g., “TRB”, “TRA”, “IGH”, “IGL”). dictates V J gene segments analyzed (e.g., TRBV TRBJ chain = “TRB”)  heatmap displays percentage TRBV-TRBJ pairing sample, providing detailed view V-J recombination landscape. Similar percentGenes(), quantitative output percentVJ() can used dimensionality reduction summarize V-J pairing patterns across samples.
code: percentVJ(combined.TCR[1:2],           chain = "TRB",           summary.fun = "percent") df.vj <- percentVJ(combined.TCR,                    chain = "TRB",                    exportTable = TRUE,                    summary.fun = "proportion") # Export proportions for PCA  # Performing PCA on the V-J pairing matrix pc.vj <- prcomp(t(df.vj))   # Getting data frame to plot from df_plot_vj <- as.data.frame(cbind(pc.vj$x[,1:2], colnames(df.vj))) colnames(df_plot_vj) <- c("PC1", "PC2", "Sample") df_plot_vj$PC1 <- as.numeric(df_plot_vj$PC1) df_plot_vj$PC2 <- as.numeric(df_plot_vj$PC2)  # Plotting the PCA results ggplot(df_plot_vj, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2)) +   geom_point(aes(fill = Sample), shape = 21, size = 5) +   guides(fill=guide_legend(title="Samples")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = hcl.colors(nrow(df_plot_vj), "inferno")) +   theme_classic() +   labs(title = "PCA of TRBV-TRBJ Gene Pairings")

title: Summarizing Repertoires
text: Another quantification composition CDR3 sequence define motifs sliding across amino acid nucleotide sequences set intervals, resulting substrings kmers. Key Parameter(s) percentKmer(): cloneCall: Defines clonal sequence grouping; accepts aa (amino acids) nt (nucleotides) motif.length: length kmer analyze. top.motifs: Displays n variable motifs function median absolute deviation. visualize percentage top 25 variable 3-mer amino acid motifs TRB chain:  perform analysis nucleotide motifs:  heatmaps generated percentKmer() illustrate relative composition frequently occurring k-mer motifs across samples groups. analysis can highlight recurrent sequence patterns CDR3, may associated specific antigen recognition, disease states, processing mechanisms. examining variable motifs, researchers can identify key sequence features differentiate immune repertoires.
code: percentKmer(combined.TCR,              cloneCall = "aa",             chain = "TRB",              motif.length = 3,              top.motifs = 25) percentKmer(combined.TCR,              cloneCall = "nt",             chain = "TRB",              motif.length = 3,              top.motifs = 25)

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: escape R package designed Easy single-cell analysis platform enrichment. tortuous acronym implies, escape designed user-friendly package gene set enrichment analysis leverages heterogeneity single-cell data. escape turns raw single-cell counts intuitive, per-cell gene-set scores single command provides plotting helpers interrogate . core workflow : Choose gene-set library (getGeneSets() list) Score cells (runEscape()) (Optional) Normalize drop-(performNormalization()) Explore built-visualization gallery information available GitHub Repo.

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: Gene set enrichment analysis begins identifying appropriate gene sets study. getGeneSets() function simplifies process extracting one gene set libraries Molecular Signature Database (MSigDB) returning GSEABase GeneSetCollection object. Note first time run getGeneSets(), downloads complete local copy gene sets, may take little . Future calls use cached version, greatly improving performance. retrieve gene set collections MSigDB, specify library libraries interest using library parameter. example, select multiple libraries, use: subcategory: Narrow selection specifying subcategories within library. Examples include “CGN”, “CGP”, “CP:BIOCARTA”, “CP:KEGG”, “GO:BP”, “IMMUNESIGDB”, etc. gene.sets: Isolate individual pathways gene sets providing specific names.
code: GS.hallmark <- getGeneSets(library = "H")

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: Several popular methods exist Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA). methods can vary underlying assumptions. escape incorporates several methods particularly advantageous single-cell RNA values:

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: method calculates enrichment score using rank-normalized approach generating empirical cumulative distribution function individual cell. enrichment score defined gene set (G) using number genes gene set (NG) total number genes (N). ES(G,S)∑=1n[PWG(G,S,)−PNG(G,S,)] ES(G,S) \sum_{= 1}^{n} [P_W^G(G,S,)-P_{NG}(G,S,)] Please see following citation information.

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: GSVA varies slightly estimating Poisson-based kernel cumulative density function. like ssGSEA, ultimate enrichment score reported based maximum value random walk statistic. GSVA appears better overall consistency runs faster ssGSEA. ESjkmax=Vjk[maxl=1,...,p|vjk(l)] ES_{jk}^{max} = V_{jk} [max_{l=1,...,p}|v_{jk}(l)] Please see following citation information.

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: contrast ssGSEA GSVA, AUCell takes gene rankings cell step-wise plots position gene gene set along y-axis. output score area curve approach. Please see following citation information.

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: UCell calculates Mann-Whitney U statistic based gene rank list. Importantly, UCell cut-ranked genes (rmaxr_{max}) 1500 - per design drop-single-cell can alter enrichment results. also substantially speeds calculations . enrichment score output calculated using complement U statistic scaled gene set size cut-. $$ U_j^` = 1-\frac{U_j}{n \bullet r_{max}} $$ Please see following citation information.

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: escape 2 major functions - first escape.matrix(), serves backbone enrichment calculations. Using count-level data supplied single-cell object matrix, escape.matrix() produce enrichment score individual cells gene sets selected output values matrix. method AUCell GSVA ssGSEA UCell groups number cells calculate . min.size minimum size detectable genes gene set. Gene sets less min.size removed calculation. normalize Use number genes gene sets cell normalize enrichment scores. default value FALSE. make.positive normalization, whether shift enrichment values positive range (TRUE) (FALSE). default value FALSE. Cautionary note: make.positive added allow differential analysis downstream enrichment methods may produce negative values. preserves log-fold change, ultimately modifies enrichment values used caution.  Multi-core support methods available BiocParallel. add cores, use argument BPPARAM escape.matrix(). use SnowParam() ’s support across platforms explicitly call 2 workers (cores).
code: enrichment.scores <- escape.matrix(scRep_example,                                     gene.sets = escape.gene.sets,                                     groups = 1000,                                     min.size = 5)  ggplot(data = as.data.frame(enrichment.scores),        mapping = aes(enrichment.scores[,1], enrichment.scores[,2])) +    geom_point() +    theme_classic() +    theme(axis.title = element_blank()) enrichment.scores <- escape.matrix(scRep_example,                                     gene.sets = escape.gene.sets,                                     groups = 1000,                                     min.size = 3,                                     BPPARAM = BiocParallel::SnowParam(workers = 2))

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: Alternatively, can use runEscape() calculate enrichment score directly attach output single-cell object. additional parameter ``runEscape new.assay.name, order save enrichment scores custom assay single-cell object. can quickly examine attached enrichment scores using visualization/workflow prefer - use just FeaturePlot() Seurat R package.
code: scRep_example <- runEscape(scRep_example,                             method = "ssGSEA",                            gene.sets = escape.gene.sets,                             groups = 1000,                             min.size = 3,                            new.assay.name = "escape.ssGSEA")  scRep_example <- runEscape(scRep_example,                             method = "UCell",                            gene.sets = escape.gene.sets,                             groups = 1000,                             min.size = 5,                            new.assay.name = "escape.UCell") #Define color palette  colorblind_vector <- hcl.colors(n=7, palette = "inferno", fixup = TRUE)  DefaultAssay(scRep_example) <- "escape.ssGSEA" FeaturePlot(scRep_example, "Proinflammatory") +    scale_color_gradientn(colors = colorblind_vector) +    theme(plot.title = element_blank())

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: Although glossed normalization can used escape.matrix() runEscape(), worth mentioning normalization can affect downstream analyses. can inherent bias enrichment values due drop single-cell expression data. Cells larger numbers features counts likely higher enrichment values. performNormalization() normalize enrichment values calculating number genes expressed gene set cell. similar normalization classic GSEA stored new assay. alternative scaling expressed gene sets use scaling factor previously calculated normal single-cell data processing quality control. can done using scale.factor argument providing vector. performNormalization() additional parameter make.positive. Across individual gene sets, negative normalized enrichment scores seen, minimum value added values. example normalized enrichment scores (accounting drop ) ranges -50 50, make.positive adjust range 0 100 (adding 50). allows compatible log2-fold change downstream, can alter enrichment score interpretation.
code: scRep_example <- performNormalization(input.data = scRep_example,                                     assay = "escape.ssGSEA",                                     gene.sets = escape.gene.sets) scRep_example <- performNormalization(input.data = scRep_example,                                     assay = "escape.ssGSEA",                                     gene.sets = escape.gene.sets,                                     scale.factor = scRep_example$nFeature_RNA)

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: number ways look enrichment values downstream runEscape() myriad plotting visualizations functions/packages single-cell data. escape include several additional plotting functions assist analysis.

title: Single-cell Gene Set Enrichment Analysis
text: can examine enrichment values across gene sets using heatmapEnrichment(). visualization return mean group.variable. default - visualizations single-cell objects use cluster assignment active identity default visualizations.  visualizations escape defined set parameters. group.grouping variable comparison. facet.Using variable facet graph separate visualizations. scale TRUE - z-transform enrichment values. FALSE - leave raw values (DEFAULT). addition, heatmapEnrichment() allows reclustering rows columns using Euclidean distance enrichment scores Ward2 methods clustering using cluster.rows cluster.columns.  visualization additional argument called **palette supplies coloring scheme used - available color palettes can viewed hcl.pals().  Alternatively, can add additional layer ggplot object returned visualizations using something like scale_fill_gradientn() continuous values scale_fill_manual() categorical variables.
code: heatmapEnrichment(scRep_example,                    group.by = "ident",                   gene.set.use = "all",                   assay = "escape.ssGSEA") heatmapEnrichment(scRep_example,                    group.by = "ident",                   assay = "escape.UCell",                   scale = TRUE,                   cluster.rows = TRUE,                   cluster.columns = TRUE) hcl.pals() ##   [1] "Pastel 1"      "Dark 2"        "Dark 3"        "Set 2"         ##   [5] "Set 3"         "Warm"          "Cold"          "Harmonic"      ##   [9] "Dynamic"       "Grays"         "Light Grays"   "Blues 2"       ##  [25] "Purple-Yellow" "Blue-Yellow"   "Green-Yellow"  "Red-Yellow"    ##  [29] "Heat"          "Heat 2"        "Terrain"       "Terrain 2"     ##  [33] "Viridis"       "Plasma"        "Inferno"       "Rocket"        ##  [61] "OrRd"          "Oranges"       "YlGn"          "YlGnBu"        ##  [65] "Reds"          "RdPu"          "PuRd"          "Purples"       ##  [69] "PuBuGn"        "PuBu"          "Greens"        "BuGn"          ##
