title: TCR Specificity Grouping with immGLIPH
text: connections data frame contains edge list representing TCR similarity network:
code: if (!is.null(res_gliph2$connections)) {   head(res_gliph2$connections) } ##                 V1               V2  type cluster_tag ## 1    CASAMGAKDGYTF CASSAKDGSSGNTIYF local    AKDG_1_4 ## 2    CASAKDGSDTQYF CASSAKDGSSGNTIYF local    AKDG_1_4 ## 3  CASSASDLAKNIQYF    CSAASDLGYTQYF local    ASDL_1_6 ## 4 CATSDAFGGAPDTQYF  CASSLDAFGTHGYTF local    DAFG_2_5 ## 5   CSASPPGDAFGYTF  CASSLDAFGTHGYTF local    DAFG_2_5 ## 6  CASSLDAFGTHGYTF  CASSLDAFGTHGYTF local    DAFG_2_5

title: TCR Specificity Grouping with immGLIPH
text: findMotifs() function searches continuous discontinuous k-mer motifs set CDR3 sequences. used internally runGLIPH() can also called independently: Including discontinuous motifs (e.g., C.S . amino acid):
code: # Extracting sequences sample_seqs <- immApex::getIR(scRep_example,                                chains = "TRB")[seq_len(200), "cdr3_aa"]  # Find all 3-mers appearing at least 5 times motifs <- findMotifs(seqs = sample_seqs, q = 3, kmer_mindepth = 5) head(motifs[order(motifs$V1, decreasing = TRUE), ]) ##    motif  V1 ## 56   CAS 164 ## 22   ASS 157 ## 31   GEL  92 ## 61   LFF  92 ## 71   ELF  90 ## 76   TGE  89 disc_motifs <- findMotifs(   seqs         = sample_seqs,   q            = 2,   kmer_mindepth = 5,   discontinuous = TRUE ) disc_only <- disc_motifs[grep("\\.", disc_motifs$motif), ] head(disc_only[order(disc_only$V1, decreasing = TRUE), ]) ##     motif  V1 ## 91     S. 391 ## 92     .S 391 ## 102    .A 344 ## 112    A. 332 ## 74     .F 322 ## 113    R. 292

title: TCR Specificity Grouping with immGLIPH
text: "custom" method allows independent control algorithmic component:
code: res_custom <- runGLIPH(   cdr3_sequences  = scRep_example,   method          = "custom",   local_method    = "fisher",   global_method   = "cutoff",   clustering_method = "GLIPH1.0",   scoring_method  = "GLIPH2.0",   sim_depth       = 100,   n_cores         = 1 )

title: TCR Specificity Grouping with immGLIPH
text: Fisher-based local method (GLIPH2), can adjust: lcminp: Maximum p-value motif enrichment (default 0.01) lcminove: Minimum fold-enrichment per motif length (default c(1000, 100, 10) 2-mers, 3-mers, 4-mers) kmer_mindepth: Minimum motif observations sample (default 3)
code: res_strict <- runGLIPH(   cdr3_sequences = scRep_example,   method         = "gliph2",   lcminp         = 0.001,   lcminove       = c(10000, 1000, 100),   sim_depth      = 100,   n_cores        = 1 )

title: TCR Specificity Grouping with immGLIPH
text: immGLIPH ships reference repertoires human mouse original GLIPH publications:

title: TCR Specificity Grouping with immGLIPH
text: plotNetwork() function creates interactive network visualization convergence groups using visNetwork package:
code: plotNetwork(   clustering_output = res_gliph2,   color_info        = "total.score",   cluster_min_size  = 3,   n_cores           = 1 )

title: TCR Specificity Grouping with immGLIPH
text: deNovoTCRs() function generates artificial CDR3β\beta sequences resemble positional amino acid composition given convergence group. can used predict novel TCR sequences similar binding characteristics:
code: de_novo <- deNovoTCRs(   convergence_group_tag = res_gliph2$cluster_properties$tag[1],   clustering_output     = res_gliph2,   sims                  = 10000,   num_tops              = 100,   make_figure           = TRUE,   n_cores               = 1 )  head(de_novo$de_novo_sequences)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: immLynx comprehensive R package bridges popular Python-based immune repertoire analysis tools Hugging Face protein language models R environment. provides unified interfaces : TCR distance calculations (tcrdist3) Sequence generation probability (OLGA) Selection inference (soNNia) TCR clustering (clusTCR) Protein language model embeddings (ESM-2) Metaclone discovery (metaclonotypist) immLynx fully compatible scRepertoire immApex ecosystem single-cell immune repertoire analysis. information available immLynx GitHub Repo.

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
code: devtools::install_github("BorchLab/immLynx")

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: immLynx uses basilisk manage Python dependencies automatically. first time use functions call Python, basilisk create isolated conda environment required packages. may take minutes first run.

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: demonstrate analysis functions immLynx, use scRepertoire example data derived T cells 4 patients acute respiratory distress. data contains paired peripheral-blood (B) bronchoalveolar lavage (L) samples across 8 distinct runs 10x Cell Ranger pipeline.

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
code: suppressMessages(library(scRepertoire)) suppressMessages(library(immLynx)) suppressMessages(library(Seurat)) suppressMessages(library(ggplot2)) suppressMessages(library(viridis)) suppressMessages(library(dplyr))  data("contig_list") combined.TCR <- combineTCR(contig_list,                            samples = c("P17B", "P17L", "P18B", "P18L",                                             "P19B","P19L", "P20B", "P20L"))  scRep_example <- readRDS("scRep_example_full.rds")  scRep_example <- combineExpression(combined.TCR,                                    scRep_example,                                    cloneCall="aa",                                    group.by = "sample",                                    proportion = TRUE)  #Filtering for single-cells with TCRs scRep_example <- subset(scRep_example,                         cells = colnames(scRep_example)[!is.na(scRep_example$CTaa)])

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: running analyses, can extract validate TCR data single-cell object:
code: # Extract beta chain data tcr_data <- extractTCRdata(scRep_example, chains = "TRB") head(tcr_data)  # Extract both chains in wide format tcr_wide <- extractTCRdata(scRep_example, chains = "both", format = "wide")  # Validate the data validation <- validateTCRdata(tcr_data) print(validation)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Get comprehensive overview TCR repertoire:
code: summary <- summarizeTCRrepertoire(scRep_example, chains = "TRB") print(summary)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Cluster TCRs based sequence similarity using clusTCR algorithm. MCL (Markov Clustering) algorithm identifies groups TCRs similar CDR3 sequences:
code: scRep_example <- runClustTCR(   scRep_example,   chains = "TRB",   method = "mcl",   inflation = 2.0 )  table(scRep_example$clustcr_TRB)

previous_headings: Analysis Functions > TCR Clustering with clusTCR
title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Different inflation parameters control granularity clustering:
code:
# MCL clustering with different inflation parameters scRep_example <- runClustTCR(scRep_example, method = "mcl", inflation = 2.0,                             column_prefix = "mcl_2") scRep_example <- runClustTCR(scRep_example, method = "mcl", inflation = 3.0,                             column_prefix = "mcl_3")  # DBSCAN clustering scRep_example <- runClustTCR(scRep_example, method = "dbscan", eps = 0.5,                              column_prefix = "dbscan")  # Compare number of clusters cat("MCL (inflation=2):", length(unique(na.omit(scRep_example$mcl_2_TRB))), "clusters
") cat("MCL (inflation=3):", length(unique(na.omit(scRep_example$mcl_3_TRB))), "clusters
") cat("DBSCAN:", length(unique(na.omit(scRep_example$dbscan_TRB))), "clusters
")

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Calculate pairwise TCR distances using tcrdist3 framework: distance matrix can also used hierarchical clustering downstream analyses:
code: dist_results <- runTCRdist(   scRep_example,   chains = "beta",   organism = "human" )  dim(dist_results$distances$pw_beta) d <- as.dist(dist_results$distances$pw_beta) hc <- hclust(d, method = "complete") clusters <- cutree(hc, k = 50)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Calculate generation probability (Pgen) TCR sequences. Pgen reflects probability given TCR sequence generated V(D)J recombination:
code: scRep_example <- runOLGA(   scRep_example,   chains = "TRB",   model = "humanTRB" )  hist(log10(scRep_example$olga_pgen_TRB),      breaks = 50,      main = "Distribution of TCR Generation Probabilities",      xlab = "log10(Pgen)")

previous_headings: Analysis Functions > Generation Probability with OLGA
title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Cells low Pgen may stronger selection rare naive repertoire:
code: ggplot(scRep_example@meta.data, aes(x = Type, y = olga_pgen_log10_TRB)) +   geom_boxplot() +   labs(title = "TCR Generation Probability by Sample Type",        x = "Sample Type",        y = "log10(Pgen)") +   theme_classic()

previous_headings: Analysis Functions > Generation Probability with OLGA
title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: OLGA can also generate random TCR sequences recombination model:
code: random_seqs <- generateOLGA(n = 100, model = "humanTRB") head(random_seqs)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Generate TCR embeddings using ESM-2 protein language models. embeddings capture biochemical structural properties CDR3 sequences:
code: scRep_example <- runEmbeddings(   scRep_example,   chains = "TRB",   model_name = "facebook/esm2_t12_35M_UR50D",   pool = "mean" )  scRep_example <- RunUMAP(scRep_example,                           reduction = "tcr_esm",                           dims = 1:30,                           reduction.name = "tcr_umap",                           reduction.key = "tcrUMAP_")  DimPlot(scRep_example, reduction = "tcr_umap")

previous_headings: Analysis Functions > Protein Language Model Embeddings
title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: ESM-2 comes different sizes. Larger models may capture nuanced biochemical properties require computational resources:
code: # Small model (35M parameters) - fast, lower quality scRep_example <- runEmbeddings(   scRep_example,   model_name = "facebook/esm2_t12_35M_UR50D",   reduction_name = "esm_small" )  # Medium model (650M parameters) - balanced scRep_example <- runEmbeddings(   scRep_example,   model_name = "facebook/esm2_t33_650M_UR50D",   reduction_name = "esm_medium" )  # Compare embeddings via UMAP scRep_example <- RunUMAP(scRep_example, reduction = "esm_small", dims = 1:30,                           reduction.name = "umap_small", reduction.key = "umapSmall_") scRep_example <- RunUMAP(scRep_example, reduction = "esm_medium", dims = 1:30,                           reduction.name = "umap_medium", reduction.key = "umapMed_")  p1 <- DimPlot(scRep_example, reduction = "umap_small") + ggtitle("ESM-2 35M") p2 <- DimPlot(scRep_example, reduction = "umap_medium") + ggtitle("ESM-2 650M") p1 + p2

previous_headings: Analysis Functions > Protein Language Model Embeddings
title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: paired alpha-beta TCR analysis:
code: scRep_example <- runEmbeddings(   scRep_example,   chains = "both",   reduction_name = "tcr_paired" )  scRep_example <- RunUMAP(scRep_example,                           reduction = "tcr_paired",                           dims = 1:30,                           reduction.name = "paired_umap",                           reduction.key = "pairedUMAP_")  DimPlot(scRep_example, reduction = "paired_umap")

previous_headings: Analysis Functions > Protein Language Model Embeddings
title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: advanced users need fine-grained control embedding process, immLynx exposes lower-level functions used internally runEmbeddings():
code: # Step 1: Load model and tokenizer model_info <- huggingModel(model_name = "facebook/esm2_t12_35M_UR50D")  # Step 2: Extract CDR3 sequences sequences <- scRep_example$CTaa sequences <- gsub("_.*", "", sequences)  # Get first chain sequences <- unique(na.omit(sequences))  # Step 3: Tokenize sequences tokenized <- tokenizeSequences(   tokenizer = model_info$tokenizer,   aa_sequences = sequences,   padding = TRUE,   truncation = TRUE )  # Step 4: Generate embeddings with custom settings embeddings <- proteinEmbeddings(   model = model_info$model,   tokenized.batch = tokenized,   pool = "mean",   prefer_device = "auto" )  dim(embeddings)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Identify TCR metaclones - groups related T cell receptors may recognize similar antigens:
code: scRep_example <- runMetaclonotypist(   scRep_example,   chains = "beta",   method = "tcrdist",   max_edits = 2,   max_dist = 20 )  table(scRep_example$metaclone)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: HLA typing data available, can test associations metaclone membership HLA alleles using Fisher’s exact test FDR correction:
code: # First run metaclonotypist metaclones <- runMetaclonotypist(scRep_example, return_seurat = FALSE)  # Create mock HLA data (replace with real HLA typing data) hla_data <- data.frame(   barcode = metaclones$barcode,   HLA_A_01_01 = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), nrow(metaclones), replace = TRUE),   HLA_A_02_01 = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), nrow(metaclones), replace = TRUE),   HLA_B_07_02 = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), nrow(metaclones), replace = TRUE) )  # Test associations hla_results <- runHLAassociation(metaclones, hla_data) head(hla_results)

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Infer selection pressures TCRs using soNNia framework. requires background set sequences comparison:
code: # Generate background sequences background <- generateOLGA(n = 10000, model = "humanTRB") write.csv(background, "background.csv", row.names = FALSE)  # Run soNNia scRep_example <- runSoNNia(   scRep_example,   chains = "TRB",   background_file = "background.csv" )

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: complete workflow combining multiple immLynx analyses scRepertoire example data:
code: library(immLynx) library(Seurat) library(ggplot2)  # Step 1: Cluster TCRs scRep_example <- runClustTCR(   scRep_example,   chains = "TRB",   method = "mcl" )  # Step 2: Calculate generation probability scRep_example <- runOLGA(   scRep_example,   chains = "TRB" )  # Step 3: Generate embeddings scRep_example <- runEmbeddings(   scRep_example,   chains = "TRB" )  # Step 4: Visualize embeddings scRep_example <- RunUMAP(   scRep_example,   reduction = "tcr_esm",   dims = 1:30,   reduction.name = "tcr_umap",   reduction.key = "tcrUMAP_" )  # Plot by cluster plot1 <- DimPlot(scRep_example,                  reduction = "tcr_umap",                  group.by = "clustcr_TRB") + NoLegend()  # Plot by Pgen plot2 <- FeaturePlot(scRep_example,                      reduction = "tcr_umap",                      features = "olga_pgen_log10_TRB") +          scale_color_viridis()  plot1 + plot2

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: immLynx clustering results can compared directly scRepertoire clonotype assignments:
code: scRep_example <- runClustTCR(scRep_example, chains = "TRB")  comparison <- table(   clustcr = scRep_example$clustcr_TRB,   clonotype = scRep_example$CTstrict )  cat("Number of clustcr clusters:", nrow(comparison), "\n") cat("Number of unique clonotypes:", ncol(comparison), "\n")

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: Export TCR data use external tools:
code: # Export TCR data in tcrdist3 format tcr_data <- extractTCRdata(scRep_example, chains = "both", format = "wide") tcrdist_format <- convertToTcrdist(tcr_data, chains = "both") write.csv(tcrdist_format, "tcr_for_tcrdist.csv", row.names = FALSE)  # Export cluster assignments clusters <- data.frame(   barcode = colnames(scRep_example),   clustcr = scRep_example$clustcr_TRB ) write.csv(clusters, "cluster_assignments.csv", row.names = FALSE)  # Export embeddings embeddings <- Embeddings(scRep_example, "tcr_esm") write.csv(embeddings, "tcr_embeddings.csv")

title: Bridging Python TCR Tools with immLynx
text: large datasets, consider strategies manage memory computation:
code: # 1. Use smaller chunk sizes for embeddings scRep_example <- runEmbeddings(   scRep_example,   chunk_size = 16,  # Smaller chunks for memory   pool = "mean" )  # 2. Sample for distance calculations # For very large datasets, calculate distances on a subset sample_cells <- sample(colnames(scRep_example), 5000) subset_obj <- subset(scRep_example, cells = sample_cells) dist_results <- runTCRdist(subset_obj)

title: Immune Reference Data with immReferent
text: immReferent package provides centralized interface downloading, managing, loading immune repertoire HLA reference sequences IMGT, IPD-IMGT/HLA, OGRDB. serves core dependency immunogenomics packages, ensuring reliable high-quality sequence access local caching reproducibility. context scRepertoire, immReferent useful : Obtaining reference gene sequences used TCR/BCR analysis Exporting formatted references tools like MiXCR, TRUST4, Cell Ranger, IgBLAST Ensuring offline reproducibility caching system

title: Immune Reference Data with immReferent
text: via Bioconductor:
code: devtools::install_github("BorchLab/immReferent") if (!require("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))     install.packages("BiocManager")  BiocManager::install("immReferent")

title: Immune Reference Data with immReferent
code: library(immReferent)

title: Immune Reference Data with immReferent
text: IPD-IMGT/HLA database provides reference sequences Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA) system.

title: Immune Reference Data with immReferent
text: immReferent automatically caches downloaded data locally. subsequent requests, cached copy loaded without network access. load strictly cache (downloads): force re-download online data updated: can change cache location session permanently via .Rprofile:
code: # List all cached IMGT files listIMGT() ##  [1] "/Users/borcherding.n/.immReferent/human/constant/imgt_human_IGHC.fasta"                                                                                                                                       ## title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: Immcantation workflow suite R packages/python scripts designed analysis B cell receptor (BCR) sequencing data. guide walk installation loading core packages required analyzing single-cell BCR data accompanying interaction scRepertoire. information Immcantation workflow available website.

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: brief overview role package plays Immcantation workflow: alakazam: package provides general tools working AIRR (Adaptive Immune Receptor Repertoire) data. includes functions data import/export, quality control, basic frequency analysis V(D)J genes clonotypes. dowser: identifying clones scoper, dowser used perform phylogenetic analysis sequences within clone. helps reconstructing lineage trees trace affinity maturation process unmutated common ancestor. scoper: Used inferring B cell clones BCR sequencing data. implements several methods partitioning sequences clonal groups based shared VJ genes junction region similarity. shazam: package focuses analysis somatic hypermutation (SHM) immunoglobulin (Ig) sequences. functions allow quantify mutation frequencies, analyze mutational spectra, measure strength nature selection pressures.
code: packages <- c("alakazam", "shazam", "scoper", "dowser")  install.packages(packages)

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
code: library(alakazam) library(dowser) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(ggtree) library(scoper) library(scRepertoire) library(shazam) library(stringr) library(Seurat) library(tibble) library(viridis)  # Using standard ggplot theme theme_vignette <- scRepertoire:::.themeRepertoire

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: Data vignette derived PMID: 33891889, examines immune dysregulation multisystem inflammatory syndrome children following COVID-19 infection. Raw data SRA downloaded re-aligned using standard Cell Ranger v9.0 workflow. processing raw FASTA files IgBLAST using provided bash script Immcantation_Bash.sh, set tab-separated value (.tsv) files. bash script automates use Change-O’s AssignGenes.py MakeDb.py tools, perform V(D)J gene assignment construct database file sample. output file interested all_contig_igblast_db-pass.tsv. first task R locate database files, load , combine single, clean data frame. following code block handles process, performing several essential quality control steps along way ensure data suitable downstream analysis.
code: # -------- Building BCR Data Base -------------------  # Recursively find  files that match the igBlast output file.list <- list.files(   path = "./Alignments",   pattern = "all_contig_igblast_db-pass.tsv",   recursive = TRUE )  # Generating Categorical Variables to use Downstream sample_id <- stringr::str_split(file.list, "/", simplify = TRUE)[, 1] subject_id <- stringr::str_remove(sample_id, "PBMC_") subject_id <- stringr::str_remove(subject_id, "_1") subject_id <- stringr::str_remove(subject_id, "_2")  # Iterative Processing and Filtering of Each Sample --- lib_data <- lapply(seq_along(file.list), function(x) {      tmp <- read.delim(paste0("./Alignments/", file.list[x]))   tmp$sample_id <- sample_id[x]   tmp$subject_id <- subject_id[x]   tmp$cell_id <- stringr::str_split(tmp$sequence_id, "_", simplify = TRUE)[, 1]   tmp$cell_id_unique <- paste0(sample_id[x], "_", tmp$cell_id)         # Data Cleaning and Filtering Steps    if (any(tmp$productive == "TRUE")) {     tmp <- tmp %>% filter(productive == TRUE)   } else if (any(tmp$productive == "T")) {     tmp <- tmp %>% filter(productive == "T")   }      # Remove cells that have more than one heavy chain contig assigned.   multi_heavy <- tmp %>%     filter(locus == "IGH") %>% # Isolate heavy chain records     count(cell_id) # Count heavy chains per cell      multi_heavy_cells <- multi_heavy %>%     filter(n > 1) %>%     pull(cell_id) # Extract the cell IDs as a vector      # Filter out all records (both heavy and light) from these problematic cells.   tmp <- tmp %>% filter(!cell_id %in% multi_heavy_cells)      # Standardize isotype calls ('c_call').   tmp$c_call <- stringr::str_split(tmp$c_call, ",", simplify = TRUE)[, 1]      # Remove any records where an isotype could not be determined.   tmp <- tmp %>% filter(c_call != "")      # Ensure all remaining cells have a paired heavy chain.   heavy_cells <- tmp %>% filter(locus == "IGH") %>% pull(cell_id)   light_cells <- tmp %>% filter(locus == "IGK" | locus == "IGL") %>% pull(cell_id)   no_heavy_cells <- setdiff(light_cells, heavy_cells)      # Remove the orphan light chain records.   tmp <- tmp %>% filter(!cell_id %in% no_heavy_cells)      return(tmp) })  # Combining all the data together bcr_db <- do.call(rbind, lib_data) saveRDS(bcr_db, "Immcantation_bcr_db.rds")

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: core task B cell repertoire analysis group sequences “clones”—sets cells originated initial B cell underwent affinity maturation. Immcantation workflow defines clones sequences use V J genes high degree similarity junction regions. define “high similarity”? need determine specific, objective distance threshold. Sequences junction similarity distance threshold considered part clone. step uses automated, data-driven approach find threshold.

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: first step calculate Hamming distance (number positions characters different) junction sequence B cell “nearest neighbor.” nearest neighbor sequence different cell similar junction.
code: # Loading Data from Above Step bcr_db <- readRDS(url("https://www.borch.dev/uploads/data/Immcantation_bcr_db.rds"))  # Split by Subject Id dist_heavy <- distToNearest(bcr_db,                              cellIdColumn = "cell_id_unique",                             first = FALSE,                             onlyHeavy = TRUE,                             fields = "subject_id",                             nproc = 2,                              progress = TRUE)

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: distribution nearest-neighbor distances typically bimodal. sharp peak small distances (sequences within clone) broader peak larger distances (sequences different clones). findThreshold function scoper package automatically finds valley two peaks. function fits Gaussian Mixture Model (method = "gmm") distance distribution. assumes distribution mix gamma distribution (clonally-related distances) normal distribution (unrelated distances).
code: # Find threshold for cloning automatically # Note: GMM fitting can occasionally fail to converge depending on the data. # If this occurs, a manual threshold (e.g., 0.15) can be used instead. threshold_output <- tryCatch(   findThreshold(dist_heavy$dist_nearest,                 progress = TRUE,                 method = "gmm",                 model = "gamma-norm",                 cutoff = "user",                 spc = 0.995),   error = function(e) NULL ) ##       STEP> Parameter initialization ##     VALUES> 6104 ## ITERATIONS> 4 ##       STEP> Fitting gamma-norm threshold_auto <- if (!is.null(threshold_output)) threshold_output@threshold else 0.15 ggplot(subset(dist_heavy, !is.na(dist_nearest)),              aes(x = dist_nearest)) +   theme_vignette() +   xlab("Hamming distance") +    ylab("Count") +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.1)) +   geom_histogram(color = "white", binwidth = 0.02) +   geom_vline(xintercept = threshold_auto, lty=2, color='red') +   facet_grid(subject_id ~ ., scales="free_y") +   theme(strip.text.y.right = element_text(angle = 0))

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: distance threshold established, can now formally partition sequences clonal lineages. step uses scoper package apply threshold assign unique clone ID sequence. visualize resulting clone size distributions, fundamental way characterize immune repertoire.

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
code: # call clones based on heavy chain # keep light chain information for subclone calling later results <- hierarchicalClones(dist_heavy,                                cell_id = 'cell_id_unique',                               threshold = threshold_auto,                               only_heavy = FALSE,                               split_light = FALSE,                                summarize_clones = TRUE,                               nproc = 2,                                verbose = TRUE) ##      MAX_N_FILTER>  0 invalid junction(s) ( # of N > 0 ) in the junction column removed. results_db <- as.data.frame(results) results_db$sequence_id <- paste0(results_db$sample_id, "_", results_db$sequence_id)

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: Now clones defined, common first analysis quantify sizes. many cells belong clone?
code: # get clone sizes per subject clone_sizes <- countClones(results_db,                            groups=c("locus", "subject_id")) clone_sizes %>%    filter(locus=="IGH") %>%   ggplot(aes(x=seq_count)) +     geom_bar(fill = "black", color = "black") +      geom_text(aes(label=seq_count, y=1), vjust=1.5, size=1) +     facet_wrap(~subject_id) +     scale_y_log10() +     scale_x_sqrt() +     theme_vignette() +     labs(x="Cells per clone",           y="# Clonal lineages")

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: Now defined clonal groups based heavy chain similarity, next steps prepare mutation analysis refine clone definitions using paired light chain information available single-cell data. involves two main goals: Reconstruct unmutated germline sequence observed BCR. baseline measure somatic hypermutation. Define subclones identifying groups cells within heavy-chain clone share light chain.

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
code: # Read in IMGT-gapped sequences references = readIMGT(dir = "~/share/germlines/imgt/human/vdj") ## [1] "Read in 1213 from 17 fasta files" results_db <- createGermlines(results_db,                                reference = references)  # Data cleaning: Some downstream functions do not tolerate NA values in columns results_db$c_call[is.na(results_db$c_call)] <- "NA"

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: key advantage single-cell data paired heavy light chain information. defined clones using heavy chain, cells within clone may use different light chains. represent distinct “subclones.” resolveLightChains function scoper identifies groupings.
code: # Split clones into subclones by light chain subclones_db <- resolveLightChains(data=results_db,                                    cell = "cell_id_unique",                                     id = "sequence_id",                                     nproc = 2)  # General Clean Up subclones_db$v_gene <- str_split(subclones_db$v_call, "[*]", simplify = TRUE)[,1] subclones_db$j_gene <- str_split(subclones_db$j_call, "[*]", simplify = TRUE)[,1] subclones_db$c_gene <- str_split(subclones_db$c_call, "[*]", simplify = TRUE)[,1] subclones_db <- subclones_db %>%   mutate(chain = ifelse(locus == "IGH", "heavy", "light"))  subclones_db %>%    group_by(subject_id, chain, c_gene) %>%    tally() %>%   ggplot(aes(x=subject_id, y=n)) +   geom_bar(aes(fill=factor(c_gene)),             stat="identity", position="stack", color = "black", lwd = 0.25) +   facet_wrap(~chain) +   scale_fill_viridis(option = "B", discrete = TRUE, name="Isotype") +    labs(x="Subject", y="# BCRs") +   theme_vignette()

what: Preparing Data for Lineage Tree Analysis
title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: ultimate goal many BCR repertoire analyses reconstruct evolutionary history B cell clone. done building phylogenetic trees trace path somatic hypermutation unmutated germline ancestor observed, mature B cells. dowser package used phylogenetic analysis, requires data specific format: ScoperClones object. step uses formatClones function perform final data cleaning create specialized object.
code: # Remove any rows where junction_length is NA, as this is a critical field. subclones_db <- filter(subclones_db, !is.na(junction_length))  # Replace NA values in key annotation columns with the string "NA". subclones_db$c_gene[is.na(subclones_db$c_gene)] <- "NA" subclones_db$v_gene[is.na(subclones_db$v_gene)] <- "NA" subclones_db$j_gene[is.na(subclones_db$j_gene)] <- "NA" subclones_db$vj_gene[is.na(subclones_db$vj_gene)] <- "NA"  vec <- str_split(subclones_db$cell_id_unique, "_", simplify = TRUE)[,3] subclones_db$timepoint <- ifelse(vec %in% c("1", "2"), vec, "1")  clones_hl <- formatClones(subclones_db,                           cell = 'cell_id_unique',                           chain = "HL",                            split_light = FALSE,                           traits = c("subject_id", "c_gene"),                           columns = c("c_gene", "v_gene", "j_gene", "vj_gene", "subject_id", "timepoint"),                           minseq = 2,                            verbose = FALSE)

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: culmination Immcantation workflow reconstruction visualization clonal lineage trees. trees map evolutionary relationships sequences clone, starting unmutated germline ancestor tracing branches somatic hypermutation class-switching occur affinity maturation. First, use getTrees() function build maximum parsimony tree clone, scale branches represent number mutations. use collapseNodes() cleanup trees scaleBranches() enhanced visual comparison tree nodes.
code: clones_hl$num_cells <- sapply(clones_hl$data, function(x) sum(x@data$collapse_count))  # --- Build trees for each clone --- trees = getTrees(clones_hl) trees <- collapseNodes(trees) trees_m = scaleBranches(trees, edge_type="mutations")

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
code: # --- Generate and Customize Tree Plots --- # Define a color palette for isotypes for consistent plotting isotypes <- c("IGHA1", "IGHA2", "IGHD", "IGHG1", "IGHG2", "IGHG3", "IGHG4", "IGHM", "Germline") col_pal <- c(viridis_pal(option = "B")(8), "grey") names(col_pal) <- isotypes  # plotTrees generates a list of ggplot objects, one for each tree. plots_m <- plotTrees(trees_m, scale=1)  names(plots_m) <- trees_m$clone_id  # --- Example: Plotting a Single Interesting Tree --- idx <- trees_m %>% slice_max(order_by = seqs, n = 1) %>% pull(clone_id) example_tree_plot <- plots_m[[idx]] max_x <- max(example_tree_plot$data$x) # Find x-axis limit  example_tree_plot +      labs(title = paste("Lineage Tree for Clone:", idx)) +     geom_tippoint(aes(color = c_gene,                        size = collapse_count),                    alpha = 0.8) +      scale_color_manual(values = col_pal, name = "Isotype") +     scale_size(name = "# Cells", range = c(2, 6)) +     xlim(0, max_x + (max_x * 0.5)) +     theme_vignette(grid_lines = "no")

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: hallmark adaptive immune response somatic hypermutation (SHM), process B cells introduce mutations antibody genes improve antigen binding. Quantifying frequency, location, type mutations primary goal BCR repertoire analysis. step uses shazam package calculate SHM explore patterns.

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: observedMutations() function workhorse SHM analysis. compares observed sequence reconstructed germline sequence (created Step 4) tallies mutations. function called multiple times calculate different metrics. compares sequence_alignment (observed sequence) germline_alignment_d_mask (unmutated ancestor). Key Parameter(s) observedMutations() regionDefinition = IMGT_VDJ: tells function use standard IMGT definitions identify Framework Regions (FWRs) Complementarity-Determining Regions (CDRs). CDRs hypervariable loops directly contact antigen, FWRs provide structural scaffold. frequency = TRUE: Calculates mutation rate (mutations per 100 base pairs). FALSE, calculates raw count mutations. combine = TRUE: calculates single mutation value across entire VDJ segment. FALSE, calculates mutations separately sub-region (e.g., mu_freq_cdr_s silent mutations CDR, mu_freq_fwr_r replacement mutations FWR, etc.). running commands, shm_db data frame populated many new columns (e.g., mu_freq, mu_count, mu_freq_cdr_s, mu_freq_cdr_r, etc) quantify SHM great detail.
code: # Detailed Mutation Counts by Region shm_db <- observedMutations(subclones_db,                             sequenceColumn = "sequence_alignment",                             germlineColumn = "germline_alignment_d_mask",                             regionDefinition = IMGT_VDJ,                             frequency = FALSE,                             combine = FALSE,                             nproc = 2)  # Total Mutation Count (Combined VDJ regions) shm_db <- observedMutations(shm_db,                             sequenceColumn = "sequence_alignment",                             germlineColumn = "germline_alignment_d_mask",                             regionDefinition = IMGT_VDJ,                             frequency = FALSE,                             combine = TRUE,                             nproc = 2)  # Detailed Mutation Frequency by Region shm_db <- observedMutations(shm_db,                             sequenceColumn = "sequence_alignment",                             germlineColumn = "germline_alignment_d_mask",                             regionDefinition = IMGT_VDJ,                             frequency = TRUE,                             combine = FALSE,                             nproc = 2)  # Total Mutation Frequency (Combined VDJ regions shm_db <- observedMutations(shm_db,                             sequenceColumn = "sequence_alignment",                             germlineColumn = "germline_alignment_d_mask",                             regionDefinition = IMGT_VDJ,                             frequency = TRUE,                             combine = TRUE,                             nproc = 2)

what: Visualizing Replacement and Silent Mutations in CDRs
title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: key way infer positive negative selection compare rate replacement (R) mutations (change amino acid sequence) silent (S) mutations (). High levels R mutations antigen-binding CDRs classic sign positive selection improved binding.   boxplot displays frequency replacement mutations specifically within CDRs heavy chains. faceted isotype (c_call), allowing compare SHM levels naive (IgM/IgD) versus mature (IgG/IgA) B cells. typically expect see higher R mutation frequencies class-switched isotypes, indicating clones undergone selection.
code: # Clean up isotype calls for plotting shm_db$c_call <- str_split(shm_db$c_call, "[*]", simplify = TRUE)[,1]  # Plot silent mutation frequency in the CDRs shm_db %>%   filter(locus == "IGH" & c_call != "NA") %>%   ggplot(aes(x = subject_id, y=mu_freq_cdr_s, fill=c_call)) +           geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0) +            labs(title = "CDR silent mutations",                 x = "Isotype", y = "Mutation frequency") +           scale_fill_viridis(option = "B", discrete = TRUE) +           facet_grid(.~c_call, scales="free_y") +       theme_vignette() +     theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1))  +    guides(fill = "none") # Plot replacement mutation frequency in the CDRs shm_db %>%   filter(locus == "IGH" & c_call != "NA") %>%   ggplot(aes(x = subject_id, y=mu_freq_cdr_r, fill=c_call)) +           geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0) +            labs(title = "CDR replacement mutations",                 x = "Isotype", y = "Mutation frequency") +           scale_fill_viridis(option = "B", discrete = TRUE) +           facet_grid(.~c_call, scales="free_y") +       theme_vignette() +     theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1))  +    guides(fill = "none")

previous_headings: Quantifying and Analyzing Somatic Hypermutation > Calculate Somatic Hypermutation (observedMutations)
title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: Finally, can look overall distribution SHM across clones get global picture repertoire’s maturity.  series density plots shows distribution SHM frequencies isotype within subject. x-axis represents mutation frequency. peak near zero indicates large population naive unmutated cells (expected IGHM IGHD). mature immune response, expect see peaks IgG IgA shifted right, indicating higher overall mutation levels.
code: # First, calculate the mean mutation frequency for each clone mut_freq_clone <- shm_db %>%   filter(locus == "IGH") %>%   group_by(clone_id, subject_id, c_call) %>%   summarise(mean_mut_freq = mean(mu_freq))  # Order isotypes for consistent plotting isotypes = c("IGHA1", "IGHA2", "IGHD", "IGHE", "IGHG1", "IGHG2", "IGHG3", "IGHG4", "IGHM") mut_freq_clone$c_call <- factor(mut_freq_clone$c_call, levels=isotypes)  # Create a density plot of mutation frequencies ggplot(mut_freq_clone %>% filter(c_call != "NA"),         aes(mean_mut_freq, fill = c_call)) +   geom_density(alpha=0.5) +   facet_grid(c_call~subject_id, scales="free") +   scale_fill_viridis(option = "B", discrete = TRUE) +    labs(x="Mean mutation frequency per clone", y="Density",        title="Somatic Hypermutation Rate by Isotype and Subject",         fill = "Isotype") +   theme_vignette(base_size = 8) +    guides(fill = "none")

title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: final step, add clonal SHM information derived Immcantation workflow Seurat object use powerful tools scRepertoire package perform integrated analyses. information upstream processing create Seurat Object can obtained R script.

what: Merging Immcantation Data into the Seurat Object
title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: first task add Immcantation-derived metadata Seurat object. key unique cell identifier (cell_id_unique) created earlier, matches cell barcodes Seurat object.
code: # Loading the Seurat Object SeuratObject <- readRDS(url("https://www.borch.dev/uploads/data/Immcantation_SeuratObject.rds"))  # Preparing Our Immcantation Clonal Info Immc.df <- shm_db %>%     filter(chain == "heavy") %>%     select(cell_id_unique,            clone_id,            subject_id,            new_mu_count = mu_count,            new_mu_freq = mu_freq,            mu_count_cdr_r,            mu_count_cdr_s,            mu_count_fwr_r,            mu_count_fwr_s,            mu_freq_cdr_r,            mu_freq_cdr_s,            mu_freq_fwr_r,            mu_freq_fwr_s) %>%     mutate(clone_id = paste0(subject_id, "_", clone_id)) %>%     column_to_rownames("cell_id_unique") %>%     as.data.frame()  # Add Metadata to Seurat Object  SeuratObject <- AddMetaData(SeuratObject, Immc.df)

what: Visualizing Clonal Distribution on the UMAP
title: Combining Immcantation and scRepertoire
text: can now immediately visualize B cell clones mutational frequency along UMAP projection, organized transcriptional similarity.
code: plot1 <- DimPlot(SeuratObject,                   label = TRUE) +              guides(color = "none") +              theme_vignette(grid_lines = "no")   plot2 <- DimPlot(SeuratObject,                   group.by = "clone_id") +              scale_color_viridis(option = "B", discrete = TRUE) +              guides(color = "none") +              theme_vignette(grid_lines = "no") +              theme(plot.title = element_blank())   plot3 <- FeaturePlot(SeuratObject,                       features = "new_mu_freq")+              scale_color_viridis(option = "B") +              guides(color = "none") +              theme_vignette(grid_lines = "no") +              theme(plot.title = element_blank())   plot1 + plot2 + plot3

title: Downstream Analysis with immunarch
text: exportClones() function can now format scRepertoire object list immunarch can immediately use. list contains two key components: $data: list data frames, data frame repertoire single sample. $meta: metadata data frame describing samples. can see, output already format required immunarch. Now can proceed downstream analysis.
code: # Export the data with write.file = FALSE to get the R object immunarch_data <- exportClones(combined,                                 format = "immunarch",                                 write.file = FALSE)  # We can inspect the structure of the output str(immunarch_data, max.level = 2) ## List of 2 ##  $ data:List of 8 ##   ..$ P17B:'data.frame': 745 obs. of  9 variables: ##   ..$ P17L:'data.frame': 2117 obs. of  9 variables: ##   ..$ P18B:'data.frame': 1254 obs. of  9 variables: ##   ..$ P18L:'data.frame': 1202 obs. of  9 variables: ##   ..$ P19B:'data.frame': 5544 obs. of  9 variables: ##   ..$ P19L:'data.frame': 1619 obs. of  9 variables: ##   ..$ P20B:'data.frame': 6087 obs. of  9 variables: ##   ..$ P20L:'data.frame': 192 obs. of  9 variables: ##  $ meta:'data.frame':    8 obs. of  1 variable: ##   ..$ Sample: chr [1:8] "P17B" "P17L" "P18B" "P18L" ...

text: Let’s perform common analyses demonstrate workflow. visualization purposes, add “SampleType” column metadata group samples origin (B blood, L lymph node).
code: # Add a grouping variable to the metadata immunarch_data$meta$Patient <- substr(immunarch_data$meta$Sample, 1, 3) immunarch_data$meta$SampleType <- substr(immunarch_data$meta$Sample, 4, 4) head(immunarch_data$meta) ##   Sample Patient SampleType ## 1   P17B     P17          B ## 2   P17L     P17          L ## 3   P18B     P18          B ## 4   P18L     P18          L ## 5   P19B     P19          B ## 6   P19L     P19          L

title: Downstream Analysis with immunarch
text: can measure similarity repertoires calculating number shared clonotypes (public clonotypes) using repOverlap(). vis() function can generate heatmap visualize pairwise overlap.
code: # Calculate overlap using the number of public clonotypes imm_ov <- repOverlap(immunarch_data$data, .method = "public", .verbose = FALSE)  # Visualize the overlap as a heatmap vis(imm_ov)

title: Downstream Analysis with immunarch
text: Next, let’s assess clonal space homeostasis, proportion repertoire occupied clones different sizes (e.g., Small, Medium, Large). repClonality() function calculates , vis() creates bar plot.
code: # Calculate clonal space homeostasis imm_hom <- repClonality(immunarch_data$data,                         .method = "homeo",                         .clone.types = c(Small = 0.0001, Medium = 0.001,                                           Large = 0.01, Hyperexpanded = 1))  # Visualize homeostasis, grouped by sample type vis(imm_hom, .by = "SampleType", .meta = immunarch_data$meta)

title: Downstream Analysis with immunarch
text: immunarch provides powerful tools analyze visualize usage V, D, J genes. , ’ll compute usage TRAV/TRBV genes visualize distribution across samples. important note, paired mode, immunarch calculates usage V genes.  can also visualize gene usage grouped metadata variable. , analyze imm_gu object using Jensen-Shannon (js) divergence correlation analysis. JS divergence measure similarity two probability distributions (lower = similar). Conversely, correlation (cor) measures linear relationship gene usage profiles different samples.
code: # Compute TRAV/TRBV gene usage imm_gu <- geneUsage(immunarch_data$data[1], "hs.trbv", .norm = TRUE)  # Visualize gene usage as a heatmap vis(imm_gu[30:60,], .grid = TRUE, .title = "TRAV/TRBV Gene Usage") imm_gu <- geneUsage(immunarch_data$data, "hs.trbv",                      .norm = T) imm_gu_js <- geneUsageAnalysis(imm_gu,                                 .method = "js",                                 .verbose = F) imm_gu_cor <- geneUsageAnalysis(imm_gu,                                  .method = "cor",                                  .verbose = F)  p1 <- vis(imm_gu_js, .title = "Gene usage JS-divergence",            .leg.title = "JS",            .text.size = 1.5) p2 <- vis(imm_gu_cor, .title = "Gene usage correlation",            .leg.title = "Cor",            .text.size = 1.5)  p1 + p2

title: Downstream Analysis with immunarch
text: immunarch scRepertoire many overlapping features, diversity. One key difference immunarch separation calculation visualization. offers immunarch users ability group calculations post-hoc fashion. can use nonparametric chao1 estimation look diversity across samples sampleType (B = Blood L = Lung).  concludes vignette integrating scRepertoire immunarch. using exportClones() function, can easily leverage extensive analytical visualization capabilities immunarch single-cell TCR sequencing data. Immunarch extensive documentation vignettes available immunarch.com.
code: div_chao <- repDiversity(immunarch_data$data, "chao1")  p1 <- vis(div_chao) p2 <- vis(div_chao, .by = c("SampleType"),            .meta = immunarch_data$meta)  p1 + p2

title: TCRpheno applied to T cell fate
text: tcrpheno R package applies logistic regression model amino acid sequences T-cell receptor complementarity-determining regions (CDRs) 1, 2, 3. model produces phenotype scores associated specific T cell fates, providing insights potential functional trajectory T cells based TCR sequences.

title: TCRpheno applied to T cell fate
text: tcrpheno package calculates four distinct scores, linked potential T cell phenotype TCRinnate: Higher scores suggest greater likelihood T cell adopting innate-like, PLZF-high phenotype, characteristic mucosal-associated invariant T (MAIT) cells invariant natural killer T (iNKT). score strongly influenced features CDR2α specific TRAV gene usage. TCR.8: Higher scores indicate predisposition towards CD8+ T cell fate CD4+ fate. TCRs high TCR.8 scores tend depletion positive charge mid-region CDR3 loops. TCRreg: Higher scores point increased probability T cell becoming regulatory T cell (Treg), encompassing CD4+ CD8+ Treg populations. associated increased hydrophobicity CDR3β CDR3α residues. TCRmem: Higher scores suggest T cell likely differentiate memory cell rather remaining naive. score reflects general propensity T-cell activation influenced features CDR3α CDR3β. Notably, higher TCRmem scores correlate increased T-cell activation even among T cells recognizing antigen correspond strength positive selection thymus.

title: TCRpheno applied to T cell fate
text: using tcrpheno, please cite article: Lagattuta, K. et al. T cell receptor sequence influences likelihood T cell memory formation. Cell Reports. 2025 Jan 28;44(1)

code: S1 <- read.csv(".../Sample1/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv") S2 <- read.csv(".../Sample2/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv") S3 <- read.csv(".../Sample3/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv") S4 <- read.csv(".../Sample4/outs/filtered_contig_annotations.csv")  contig_list <- list(S1, S2, S3, S4)

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: Beyond default 10x Genomics Cell Ranger pipeline outputs, scRepertoire supports various single-cell immune receptor sequencing formats loadContigs() function.

what: Supported Formats and Expected File Names
title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: 10X: “filtered_contig_annotations.csv” AIRR: “airr_rearrangement.tsv” BD: “Contigs_AIRR.tsv” Dandelion: “all_contig_dandelion.tsv” Immcantation: “_data.tsv” (similar) JSON: “.json” MiXCR: “clones.tsv” ParseBio: “barcode_report.tsv” TRUST4: “barcode_report.tsv” WAT3R: “barcode_results.csv” Key Parameter(s) loadContigs() input: directory path containing contig files (function recursively search) list/data frame pre-loaded contig data. format: string specifying data format (e.g., 10X, TRUST4, WAT3R). set “auto”, function attempt automatically detect format based file names data structure. can provide loadContigs() directory sequencing experiments located, recursively load process contig data based file names: Alternatively, loadContigs() can given list pre-loaded data frames process contig data based specified format:
code: # Directory example contig.output <- c("~/Documents/MyExperiment") contig.list <- loadContigs(input = contig.output,                             format = "TRUST4") # List of data frames example S1 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample1/outs/barcode_results.csv") S2 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample2/outs/barcode_results.csv") S3 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample3/outs/barcode_results.csv") S4 <- read.csv("~/Documents/MyExperiment/Sample4/outs/barcode_results.csv")  contig.list <- list(S1, S2, S3, S4) contig.list <- loadContigs(input = contig.list,                             format = "WAT3R")

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: combineTCR() function processes list TCR sequencing results, consolidating level individual cell barcodes. handles potential issues repeated barcodes adding prefixes samples ID parameters. output includes combined reads clone calls nucleotide sequence (CTnt), amino acid sequence (CTaa), VDJC gene sequence (CTgene), combination nucleotide gene sequence (CTstrict). Key Parameter(s) combineTCR() input.data: list filtered contig annotations (e.g., filtered_contig_annotations.csv 10x Cell Ranger) outputs loadContigs(). samples: Labels samples (recommended). ID: Additional sample labels (optional). removeNA: TRUE, removes cell barcode NA value least one chain (default FALSE). removeMulti: TRUE, removes cell barcode two immune receptor chains (default FALSE). filterMulti: TRUE, isolates top two expressed chains cell barcodes multiple chains (default FALSE). filterNonproductive: TRUE, removes non-productive chains variable exists contig data (default TRUE). combine TCR contigs contig_list apply sample prefixes: combineTCR() essential first step organizing raw TCR sequencing data structured format scRepertoire analyses. allows flexible handling single paired chains, barcode disambiguation, initial filtering, producing list data frames row represents single cell associated TCR clonotypes.
code: combined.TCR <- combineTCR(contig_list,                             samples = c("P17B", "P17L", "P18B", "P18L",                                              "P19B","P19L", "P20B", "P20L"),                            removeNA = FALSE,                             removeMulti = FALSE,                             filterMulti = FALSE)  head(combined.TCR[[1]]) ## 5  P17B_AAACCTGCAGGCGATA-1   P17B      TRAV12-1.TRAJ9.TRAC     CVVSDNTGGFKTIF ## 7  P17B_AAACCTGCATGAGCGA-1   P17B      TRAV12-1.TRAJ9.TRAC     CVVSDNTGGFKTIF ## 9  P17B_AAACGGGAGAGCCCAA-1   P17B        TRAV20.TRAJ8.TRAC      CAVRGEGFQKLVF ## 10 P17B_AAACGGGAGCGTTTAC-1   P17B      TRAV12-1.TRAJ9.TRAC     CVVSDNTGGFKTIF ##                                                  cdr3_nt1 ## 1                       TGTGGGTGTTCTAACGACTACAAGCTCAGCTTT ## title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: combineBCR() function primary tool processing raw B cell receptor contig data format ready analysis. analogous combineTCR() includes specialized logic handling complexities BCRs, somatic hypermutation. function consolidates contigs single data frame per sample, row representing unique cell. default (call.related.clones = TRUE), combineBCR() groups B cells clones based similarity CDR3 sequences.

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: Internally calling clonalCluster() build network related sequences. Using threshold parameter define connections. threshold normalized Levenshtein distance, value closer 1.0 requires higher sequence similarity. default 0.85 good starting point. Assigning cluster-based ID CTstrict column. Additionally, group.argument allows constrain clustering analysis occur within distinct categories metadata. example, using group.= "sample" ensures sequences different samples never compared clustered together, even identical. Key Parameter(s) combineBCR() call.related.clones: TRUE (default), uses clonalCluster() identify related clones based sequence similarity. group.: column header used group clones clustering (NULL, clusters calculated across samples). threshold: similarity threshold clonalCluster() (default: 0.85). dist_type: metric calculating difference: “levenshtein” (default), “hamming”, “nw” (Needleman-Wunsch), “sw” (Smith-Waterman). dist_mat: substitution matrix used dist_type “nw” “sw” (e.g., “BLOSUM62”, “PAM30”) normalize: handle threshold: “none”, “length”, “maxlen”. chain: chain use clustering call.related.clones = TRUE (default: ). sequence: sequence type (nt aa) clustering (default: nt). use.V, use.J: TRUE, sequences must share V/J gene clustered (default: TRUE) cluster.method: clustering algorithm apply edit-distanc network (default: components). First, load example BCR contig data: , combine BCR contigs using default similarity clustering:
code: # Load example BCR contig data BCR.contigs <- read.csv("https://www.borch.dev/uploads/contigs/b_contigs.csv") # Combine using the default similarity clustering combined.BCR.clustered <- combineBCR(BCR.contigs,                                       samples = "Patient1",                                       threshold = 0.85)  # The CTstrict column contains cluster IDs (e.g., "cluster.1") head(combined.BCR.clustered[[1]][, c("barcode", "CTstrict", "IGH", "cdr3_aa1")]) ## title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: combineBCR() includes several arguments filter clean contig data processing. * filterNonproductive = TRUE (Default): Removes contigs classified productive, ensuring functional receptor chains included analysis. * filterMulti = TRUE (Default): cells one heavy light chain detected, automatically selects chain highest UMI count (read abundance) discards others. helps resolve cellular multiplets technical artifacts. example applying filters (though default): combineBCR() designed processing B cell repertoire data, going beyond simple contig aggregation incorporate advanced clustering based CDR3 sequence similarity. enables identification clonally related B cells, crucial studying B cell development, affinity maturation, humoral immune responses. filtering options ensure quality interpretability processed data.

text: Likewise, can remove specific list elements combineTCR() combineBCR() using subsetClones() function. order subset, need identify column header like use subsetting (name) specific values include (variables). title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: assigning clone barcode, can export clonal information using exportClones() save later use integrate bioinformatics pipelines. function supports various output formats tailored different analytical needs. Key Parameter(s) exportClones() * format: desired output format clonal data. * airr: Exports data Adaptive Immune Receptor Repertoire (AIRR) Community-compliant format, row representing single receptor chain. * immunarch: Exports list containing data frame metadata formatted use immunarch package. * paired: Exports data frame paired chain information (amino acid, nucleotide, genes) per barcode. default. * TCRMatch: Exports data frame specifically TCRMatch algorithm, containing TRB chain amino acid sequence clonal frequency. * tcrpheno: Exports data frame compatible tcrpheno pipeline, TRA TRB chains separate columns. * write.file: TRUE (default), saves output CSV file. FALSE, returns data frame list R environment. * dir: directory output file saved. Defaults current working directory. * file.name: name CSV file saved. export combined clonotypes paired data frame save specified directory: return immunarch-formatted data frame directly R environment without saving file:

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: vizGenes() function offers highly adaptable approach visualizing relative usage TCR BCR genes. acts versatile alias percentGeneUsage(), allowing comparisons across different chains, scaling values, selection bar charts heatmaps. x.axis: Specifies gene segment display along x-axis (e.g., “TRBV”, “TRBD”, “IGKJ”). Another gene segment (e.g., “TRAV”, “TRBJ”) paired gene analysis. x.axis y.axis gene segments, vizGenes() internally calls percentGeneUsage() genes = c(x.axis, y.axis), resulting heatmap. categorical variable (e.g., “sample”, “orig.ident”) visualize gene usage across different groups. y.axis categorical variable, vizGenes() maps group.parameter percentGeneUsage(), creating facets category. “barplot”: Ideal visualizing distribution single gene segment. “heatmap”: Suitable single gene usage (group.y.axis categorical variable) paired gene analysis. summary.fun: (Inherited percentGeneUsage) Defines statistic display: “percent” (default), “proportion”, “count”. implicitly handles scaling values.  plot shows proportion TRBV gene segment observed across entire combined.TCR dataset. Since y.axis NULL, samples grouped list element combined.TCR data. vizGenes() particularly useful examining gene pairings. Let’s look differences TRBV TRBJ usage peripheral blood lung samples dataset. ’ll subset combined.TCR analysis.   examples, providing x.axis y.axis gene segments (“TRBV” “TRBJ”), vizGenes() automatically performs paired gene analysis, generating heatmap intensity reflects percentage V-J pairing. Beyond V-J pairings within single chain, vizGenes() can also visualize gene usage across different chains. instance, examine TRBV TRAV pairings patient P17’s samples:
code: vizGenes(combined.TCR,          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = NULL, # No specific y-axis variable, will group all samples          plot = "barplot",          summary.fun = "proportion") # Peripheral Blood Samples vizGenes(combined.TCR[c("P17B", "P18B", "P19B", "P20B")],          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = "TRBJ",          plot = "heatmap",          summary.fun = "percent") # Display percentages # Lung Samples vizGenes(combined.TCR[c("P17L", "P18L", "P19L", "P20L")],          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = "TRBJ",          plot = "heatmap",          summary.fun = "percent") # Display percentages vizGenes(combined.TCR[c("P17B", "P17L")],          x.axis = "TRBV",          y.axis = "TRAV",          plot = "heatmap",          summary.fun = "count")

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: percentGenes() function specialized alias percentGeneUsage() designed quantify usage single V, D, J gene locus specified immune receptor chain. default, returns heatmap visualization. Key Parameters percentGenes(): chain: Specifies immune receptor chain (e.g., “TRB”, “TRA”, “IGH”, “IGL”). gene: Indicates gene locus quantify: “Vgene”, “Dgene”, “Jgene”. group., order., summary.fun, exportTable, palette: parameters directly passed percentGeneUsage(). quantify visualize percentage TRBV gene usage across samples:  generates heatmap showing percentage TRBV gene segment within sample, allowing easy visual comparison gene usage profiles across samples. raw data returned percentGenes() (exportTable = TRUE) can powerful input downstream analysis, dimensionality reduction techniques like Principal Component Analysis. allows summarize complex gene usage patterns identify samples similar distinct repertoires.  PCA plot visually clusters samples based TRBV gene usage profiles, helping identify underlying patterns relationships samples.
code: percentGenes(combined.TCR,              chain = "TRB",              gene = "Vgene",              summary.fun = "percent") df.genes <- percentGenes(combined.TCR,                          chain = "TRB",                          gene = "Vgene",                          exportTable = TRUE,                          summary.fun = "proportion")   # Performing PCA on the gene usage matrix pc <- prcomp(t(df.genes) )  # Getting data frame to plot from df_plot <- as.data.frame(cbind(pc$x[,1:2], colnames(df.genes))) colnames(df_plot) <- c("PC1", "PC2", "Sample") df_plot$PC1 <- as.numeric(df_plot$PC1) df_plot$PC2 <- as.numeric(df_plot$PC2)  ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2)) +   geom_point(aes(fill = Sample), shape = 21, size = 5) +   guides(fill=guide_legend(title="Samples")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = hcl.colors(nrow(df_plot), "inferno")) +   theme_classic() +   labs(title = "PCA of TRBV Gene Usage")

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: percentVJ() function another specialized alias percentGeneUsage(), specifically designed quantify proportion percentage V J gene segments paired together within individual repertoires. always produces heatmap visualization. Key Parameters percenVJ(): chain: Specifies immune receptor chain (e.g., “TRB”, “TRA”, “IGH”, “IGL”). dictates V J gene segments analyzed (e.g., TRBV TRBJ chain = “TRB”)  heatmap displays percentage TRBV-TRBJ pairing sample, providing detailed view V-J recombination landscape. Similar percentGenes(), quantitative output percentVJ() can used dimensionality reduction summarize V-J pairing patterns across samples.
code: percentVJ(combined.TCR[1:2],           chain = "TRB",           summary.fun = "percent") df.vj <- percentVJ(combined.TCR,                    chain = "TRB",                    exportTable = TRUE,                    summary.fun = "proportion") # Export proportions for PCA  # Performing PCA on the V-J pairing matrix pc.vj <- prcomp(t(df.vj))  # Getting data frame to plot from df_plot_vj <- as.data.frame(cbind(pc.vj$x[,1:2], colnames(df.vj))) colnames(df_plot_vj) <- c("PC1", "PC2", "Sample") df_plot_vj$PC1 <- as.numeric(df_plot_vj$PC1) df_plot_vj$PC2 <- as.numeric(df_plot_vj$PC2)  # Plotting the PCA results ggplot(df_plot_vj, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2)) +   geom_point(aes(fill = Sample), shape = 21, size = 5) +   guides(fill=guide_legend(title="Samples")) +   scale_fill_manual(values = hcl.colors(nrow(df_plot_vj), "inferno")) +   theme_classic() +   labs(title = "PCA of TRBV-TRBJ Gene Pairings")

Key Parameter(s) combineExpression() input.data: product combineTCR(), combineBCR(), list containing . sc.data: Seurat Single-Cell Experiment (SCE) object attach clonal data . proportion: TRUE (default), calculates proportion clone; FALSE, calculates total frequency. cloneSize: Bins grouping based proportion frequency. proportion FALSE cloneSize bins set high enough, upper limit automatically adjust. filterNA: Method subset Seurat/SCE object barcodes without clone information. addLabel: Adds label frequency header, useful trying multiple group.variables recalculating frequencies subsetting. can look default cloneSize groupings using Single-Cell Experiment object just created, group.set sample variable used combineTCR():  Alternatively, want cloneSize based frequency clone, can set proportion = FALSE need change cloneSize bins integers. haven’t inspected clone data, setting upper limit clonal frequency might difficult; combineExpression() automatically adjust upper limit fit distribution frequencies.

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: expa - Clonal Expansion. Measures degree clonal proliferation within given cell cluster. calculated 1 - normalized Shannon entropy, higher value indicates clones dominate cluster. migr - Cross-tissue Migration. Quantifies movement clonal T cells different tissues, defined type parameter. index based entropy single clonotype’s distribution across specified tissues. tran - State Transition. Measures developmental transition T cell clones different functional clusters. index calculated entropy single clonotype’s distribution across cell clusters identified data. Key Parameters StartracDiversity() type: variable metadata provides tissue type. group.: column header metadata group analysis (e.g., “sample”, “treatment”). default, StartracDiversity() calculate three indices (expa, migr, tran) cluster group define. provides comprehensive overview clonal dynamics dataset. output three-paneled plot, panel representing one index. example data, type corresponds “Type” column, includes “P” (peripheral blood) “L” (lung) classifiers. analysis grouped “Patient” column.
code: # Calculate and plot all three STARTRAC indices StartracDiversity(scRep_example,                    type = "Type",                    group.by = "Patient")

code: # Calculate and plot only the clonal expansion index StartracDiversity(scRep_example,                    type = "Type",                    group.by = "Patient",                   index = "expa")

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: new metric proposed Massimo et al, clonalBias(), like STARTRAC, clonal metric seeks quantify individual clones skewed towards specific cellular compartment cluster. clone bias 1 indicates clone composed cells single compartment cluster, clone bias 0 matches background subtype distribution. Please read cite linked manuscript using clonalBias() Key Parameter(s) clonalBias() group.: column header metadata bias based . split.: variable use calculating baseline frequencies (e.g., “Type” lung vs peripheral blood comparison) n.boots: Number bootstraps downsample. min.expand: Clone frequency cut-purpose comparison (default = 10). calculate plot clonal bias using aa clone calls, splitting “Patient” grouping “seurat_clusters”, minimum expansion 5 10 bootstraps:
code: clonalBias(scRep_example,             cloneCall = "aa",             split.by = "Patient",             group.by = "seurat_clusters",            n.boots = 10,             min.expand =5)

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: clonalCluster() function provides powerful method group clonotypes based sequence similarity. calculates edit distance CDR3 sequences uses information build network, identifying closely related clusters T B cell receptors. functionality allows nuanced definition “clone” extends beyond identical sequence matches.

previous_headings: Clustering by Edit Distance > clonalCluster: Cluster by Sequence Similarity
title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: clustering process follows key steps: Sequence Selection: function selects either nucleotide (sequence = "nt") amino acid (sequence = "aa") CDR3 sequences specified chain. Distance Calculation: calculates edit distance pairs sequences. default, also requires sequences share V gene (use.v = TRUE). Network Construction: edge created two sequences meet similarity threshold, forming network graph. Clustering: graph-based clustering algorithm run identify connected components communities within network. default, identifies directly indirectly connected sequences single cluster (cluster.method = "components"). Output: function can either add resulting cluster IDs input object, return igraph object network analysis, export sparse adjacency matrix.

previous_headings: Clustering by Edit Distance > clonalCluster: Cluster by Sequence Similarity
title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: behavior threshold parameter critical controlling cluster granularity: Normalized Similarity (threshold < 1): threshold value 0 1 (e.g., 0.85), represents normalized edit distance (Levenshtein distance / mean sequence length). higher value corresponds stricter similarity requirement. useful comparing sequences varying lengths. Raw Edit Distance (threshold >= 1): threshold whole number (e.g., 2), represents maximum raw edit distance allowed. lower value corresponds stricter similarity requirement. useful want allow specific number mutations. Key Parameter(s) clonalCluster() sequence: Specifies whether cluster based aa (amino acid) nt (nucleotide) sequences. threshold: similarity threshold clustering. Values < 1 normalized similarity, values >= 1 raw edit distance. group.: column header metadata lists group analysis (e.g., “sample”, “treatment”). NULL, clusters calculated across sequences. use.V: TRUE, sequences must share V gene clustered together. use.J: TRUE, sequences must share J gene clustered together. cluster.method: clustering algorithm use. Defaults components, finds connected subgraphs. cluster.prefix: character prefix add cluster names (e.g., “cluster.”). exportGraph: TRUE, returns igraph object sequence network. exportAdjMatrix: TRUE, returns sparse adjacency matrix (dgCMatrix) network.

previous_headings: Clustering by Edit Distance > clonalCluster: Cluster by Sequence Similarity
title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: run clustering first two samples TRA chain, using amino acid sequences normalized similarity threshold 0.85:
code: # Run clustering on the first two samples for the TRA chain sub_combined <- clonalCluster(combined.TCR[c(1,2)],                                chain = "TRA",                                sequence = "aa",                                threshold = 0.85)  # View the new cluster column head(sub_combined[[1]][, c("barcode", "TCR1", "TRA.Cluster")]) ##                   barcode TCR1 TRA.Cluster ## 1 P17B_AGCGGTCCAAAGGAAG-1 <NA>        <NA> ## 2 P17B_GGCTCGAGTCGCGGTT-1 <NA>        <NA> ## 3 P17B_CGCGTTTTCGGCTACG-1 <NA>        <NA> ## 4 P17B_CACCAGGGTTCCTCCA-1 <NA>        <NA> ## 5 P17B_TCTATTGCAGGTGCCT-1 <NA>        <NA> ## 6 P17B_GCTGGGTGTACGAAAT-1 <NA>        <NA>

text: can calculate clusters based specific metadata variables within single-cell object using group.parameter. useful analyzing clusters per-sample per-patient basis without subsetting data first. First, add patient type information scRep_example Seurat object: Now, run clustering scRep_example Seurat object, grouping calculations “Patient”:
code: #Adding patient information scRep_example$Patient <- substr(scRep_example$orig.ident, 1,3)  #Adding type information scRep_example$Type <- substr(scRep_example$orig.ident, 4,4) # Run clustering, but group calculations by "Patient" scRep_example <- clonalCluster(scRep_example,                                 chain = "TRA",                                 sequence = "aa",                                 threshold = 0.85,                                 group.by = "Patient")  #Define color palette  num_clusters <- length(unique(na.omit(scRep_example$TRA.Cluster))) cluster_colors <- hcl.colors(n = num_clusters, palette = "inferno")  # Bug in Seurat 5.3.1 no longer handles NA for DimPlot # Will Update in Future Commits  # DimPlot(scRep_example, group.by = "TRA.Cluster") + #  scale_color_manual(values = cluster_colors, na.value = "grey") +  #  NoLegend()

previous_headings: Clustering by Edit Distance > clonalCluster: Cluster by Sequence Similarity
title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: Instead modifying input object, clonalCluster() can export underlying network structure advanced analysis. Set exportGraph = TRUE get igraph object consisting networks barcodes indicated clustering scheme.
code: set.seed(42) #Clustering Patient 19 samples igraph.object <- clonalCluster(combined.TCR[c(5,6)],                                chain = "TRB",                                sequence = "aa",                                group.by = "sample",                                threshold = 0.85,                                 exportGraph = TRUE)  #Setting color scheme col_legend <- factor(igraph::V(igraph.object)$group) col_samples <- hcl.colors(2,"inferno")[as.numeric(col_legend)] color.legend <- factor(unique(igraph::V(igraph.object)$group)) sample.vertices <- V(igraph.object)[sample(length(igraph.object), 500)]  subgraph.object <- induced_subgraph(igraph.object, vids = sample.vertices) V(subgraph.object)$degrees <- igraph::degree(subgraph.object) edge_alpha_color <- adjustcolor("gray", alpha.f = 0.3)  #Plotting plot(subgraph.object,      layout = layout_nicely(subgraph.object),      vertex.label = NA,      vertex.size = sqrt(igraph::V(subgraph.object)$degrees),       vertex.color = col_samples[sample.vertices],      vertex.frame.color = "white",       edge.color = edge_alpha_color,      edge.arrow.size = 0.05,      edge.curved = 0.05,       margin = -0.1) legend("topleft",         legend = levels(color.legend),         pch = 16,         col = unique(col_samples),         bty = "n")

title: Starting work with scRepertoire.
text: default cluster.method = "components" robust, can use algorithms igraph package, walktrap louvain, potentially uncover different community structures. Overall, clonalCluster() versatile function defining analyzing clonal relationships based sequence similarity. allows researchers move beyond exact sequence matches, providing comprehensive understanding clonal families. ability customize parameters like threshold, chain selection, group.ensures adaptability diverse research questions. Furthermore, option export igraph objects sparse adjacency matrices provides advanced users tools -depth network analysis.
code: # Cluster using the walktrap algorithm graph_walktrap <- clonalCluster(combined.TCR[c(1,2)],                                 cluster.method = "walktrap",                                 exportGraph = TRUE)  # Compare the number of clusters found length(unique(V(graph_walktrap)$cluster)) ## [1] 419

title: A toolkit for single-cell immune receptor profiling
text: Single-cell sequencing emerging technology field immunology oncology allows researchers couple RNA quantification modalities, like immune cell receptor profiling level individual cell. Unlike transcriptomic field, lack options software allow single-cell immune receptor profiling. Enabling users easily combine RNA immune profiling, scRepertoire framework supports 10x, AIRR, BD, MiXCR, TRUST4, WAT3R single-cell clonal formats interaction popular R-based single-cell data pipelines.

title: Calculate Startrac-based Diversity Indices — StartracDiversity
text: function utilizes STARTRAC approach calculate T cell diversity metrics based work Zhang et al. (2018, Nature) PMID: 30479382. can compute three distinct indices: clonal expansion (expa), cross-tissue migration (migr), state transition (tran).

title: Calculate Startrac-based Diversity Indices — StartracDiversity
code: StartracDiversity(   sc.data,   clone.call = NULL,   chain = "both",   index = c("expa", "migr", "tran"),   type = NULL,   group.by = NULL,   pairwise = NULL,   export.table = NULL,   palette = "inferno",   cloneCall = NULL,   exportTable = NULL,   ... )

title: Calculate Startrac-based Diversity Indices — StartracDiversity
text: sc.data single-cell object combineExpression(). SCE objects, cluster variable must meta data "cluster". clone.call Defines clonal sequence grouping. Accepted values : gene (VDJC genes), nt (CDR3 nucleotide sequence), aa (CDR3 amino acid sequence), strict (VDJC + nt). custom column header can also used. chain TCR/BCR chain use. Use include chains (e.g., TRA/TRB). Accepted values: TRA, TRB, TRG, TRD, IGH, IGL, IGK, Light (light chains), (TRA/B Heavy/Light). index character vector specifying indices calculate. Options: "expa", "migr", "tran". Default three. type metadata variable specifies tissue type migration analysis. group.column header metadata lists group analysis (e.g., "sample", "treatment"). NULL, data analyzed list element active identity case single-cell objects. pairwise metadata column used pairwise comparisons. Set type variable pairwise migration "cluster" pairwise transition. export.table TRUE, returns data frame matrix results instead plot. palette Colors use visualization - input hcl.pals. cloneCall Use clone.call instead. exportTable Use export.table instead. ... Additional arguments passed ggplot theme

title: Calculate Startrac-based Diversity Indices — StartracDiversity
text: ggplot object visualizing STARTRAC diversity metrics data.frame exportTable = TRUE.

title: Calculate Startrac-based Diversity Indices — StartracDiversity
text: function requires type variable metadata, specifies tissue origin categorical variable migration analysis. Indices: expa (Clonal Expansion): Measures extent clonal proliferation within T cell cluster. calculated 1 - normalized Shannon entropy. higher value indicates greater expansion clones. migr (Cross-Tissue Migration): Quantifies movement clonal T cells across different tissues (defined type parameter). based entropy clonotype's distribution across tissues. tran (State Transition): Measures developmental transition clonal T cells different functional clusters. based entropy  clonotype's distribution across clusters. Pairwise Analysis: pairwise parameter enables calculation migration transition specific pairs tissues clusters, respectively. migration (index = "migr"), set pairwise type column (e.g., pairwise = "Type"). transition (index = "tran"), set pairwise "cluster".

title: Calculate Startrac-based Diversity Indices — StartracDiversity
text: Liangtao Zheng

title: Calculate Startrac-based Diversity Indices — StartracDiversity
code: # Getting the combined contigs combined <- combineTCR(contig_list,                          samples = c("P17B", "P17L", "P18B", "P18L",                                      "P19B","P19L", "P20B", "P20L"))  # Getting a sample of a Seurat object scRep_example  <- get(data("scRep_example")) scRep_example  <- combineExpression(combined, scRep_example) scRep_example$Patient <- substring(scRep_example$orig.ident,1,3) scRep_example$Type <- substring(scRep_example$orig.ident,4,4)   # Calculate a single index (expansion) StartracDiversity(scRep_example,                    type = "Type",                    group.by = "Patient",                   index = "expa")                     # Calculate pairwise transition  StartracDiversity(scRep_example,                    type = "Type",                    group.by = "Patient",                   index = "tran",                   pairwise = "cluster")
